3-1.q: When properly developed and administered, budgets provide the following advantages to the organization except to
o: promote the efficient allocation of resources.
motivate managers and other employees.
provide a structure for measuring performance.
ensure that the organization makes a profit.
3-1.e: Even the best budget cannot ensure that an organization will make a profit.

wrongWhy.0: This is one of the benefits of budgeting.
wrongWhy.1: This is one of the benefits of budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is one of the benefits of budgeting.

3-1.q: The use of budgetary slack does not allow the preparer to
o: Increase the probability of achieving budgeted performance.
Project actual expenses.
Be flexible under unexpected circumstances.
Use the budget to control subordinate performance.
3-1.e: When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either revenues are understated or expenses are overstated, or both. A budget should be able to be used as a control tool to compare actual performance results with budgeted performance. When the budget is too easily achieved, though, it is difficult for managers to properly evaluate the performance of subordinates. Instead, the budget should set the performance standards at a high but attainable level.

wrongWhy.0: The use of budgetary slack does increase the probability of achieving budgeted performance. Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. Thus, it is easy to attain budgeted performance. Actual revenues will probably be higher than budgeted revenues and actual expenses will probably be lower than budgeted expenses, so actual net income will probably be higher than budgeted net income.
wrongWhy.1: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. Building in budgetary slack does not prevent the manager from making a projection of actual expenses. The manager can and no doubt will make a projection of expenses. However, instead of using his or her projection of expenses as the budgeted expenses, the preparer would submit a higher budgeted expense amount. In that way, the preparer would be attempting to "guarantee" that actual expenses would come in below the budgeted amount.
wrongWhy.2: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. The use of budgetary slack does allow the preparer to be flexible under unexpected circumstances because, for example, if actual expenses turn out to be higher than the manager expected, actual expenses may still be less than the budgeted expenses.

3-1.q: In developing the budget for the next year, which one of the following approaches would produce the greatest amount of positive motivation and goal congruence?
o: Permit the divisional manager to develop the goal for the division that in the manager's view will generate the greatest amount of profits.
Have the divisional and senior management jointly develop goals and objectives while constructing the corporation's overall plan of operation.
Have the divisional and senior management jointly develop goals and the divisional manager develop the implementation plan.
Have senior management develop the overall goals and permit the divisional manager to determine how these goals will be met.
3-1.e: With open communication between the division manager and senior management all of the options can be discussed and a mutually acceptable budget can be developed. Once this budget is developed, the implementation is best left to the division manager because the division manager understands the process, employees, and environment better than senior management can, as senior management is not actively involved in the day to day operations.

wrongWhy.0: While the manager may view his budget for the division as generating the most profit, generally he/she is unaware of what is going on elsewhere within the organization. Without this knowledge, the division manager may be headed in a different direction than what is necessary for organization wide success.
wrongWhy.1: Senior management should prepare the corporation's overall plan prior to budget development. The corporate plan is likely to be longer term and include elements both external and non-financial that a budget does not address.
wrongWhy.3: When senior management develops goals they can miss opportunities or issues known by the division manager. Rarely does one entity fully understand everything that happens with other entities. Communication is essential.

3-1.q: The process of creating a formal plan and translating goals into a quantitative format is
o: Process costing.
Budgeting.
Budget manual preparation.
Job-order costing.
3-1.e: A budget is a realistic plan for the future expressed in quantitative terms.

wrongWhy.0: Process costing is the method by which costs are assigned to individual products when the products are all relatively similar (homogeneous) and are mass-produced.
wrongWhy.2: A budget manual details the budgeting process.
wrongWhy.3: Job-order costing is the method by which all of the costs associated with a specific job (or client) are accumulated and charged to that job (or client). The costs are accumulated on what is called a job-cost sheet.

3-1.q: All of the following are advantages of top-down budgeting as opposed to participatory budgeting, except that it:
o: May limit the acceptance of proposed goals and objectives.
Reduces the time required for budgeting.
Increases coordination of divisional objectives.
Facilitates implementation of strategic plans.
3-1.e: This is not an advantage of top-down budgeting, although it is a true statement. When senior management determines the budget without input from the departments being held to that budget, employees are less likely to feel they can / want to achieve the target. It wasn't their idea and no one listened to them.

wrongWhy.1: This is an advantage of top-town budgeting. When the budget is dictated from above, departmental managers do not need to spend time putting together their plans and ideas for the future. Furthermore, there is no need for lower level managers to make adjustments to the first draft of the budget, as is done with bottom up budgeting after senior management has examined the first draft budgets of the lower level managers. The result is that less time is required for the budgeting process.
wrongWhy.2: This is an advantage of top-down budgeting. Frequently, departmental managers have blinders on and are unaware of what is happening in other areas of the company. Senior management has a better perspective of the big picture and what will be necessary to achieve overall organizational objectives.
wrongWhy.3: This is an advantage of top-town budgeting. Strategic plans are long-term in nature and require a good deal or coordination in order to be successful. Senior management's job is to position each division and department appropriately to fulfill their role in the long term organizational plan. Top-down budgeting achieves this more readily than bottom-up budgeting, because senior management has the required long-term perspective and lower level managers do not.

3-1.q: An advantage of participatory budgeting is that it
o: reduces the effect on the budgetary process of employee biases.
yields information known to management but not to employees.
minimizes the cost of developing budgets.
encourages acceptance of the budget by employees.
3-1.e: Participative (bottom-up) budget development involves all the people affected by the budget, even lower-level employees, and it involves negotiation between lower-level managers and senior management. A participative budget is more likely to gain employee commitment to fulfill budgetary goals than one where the affected people have no input. People are more willing to devote extra effort to attain goals they perceive as their own.

wrongWhy.0: Participative (bottom-up) budget development does not reduce the effect on the budgetary process of employee biases. Participative budget development involves all the people affected by the budget, even lower-level employees, and it involves negotiation between lower-level managers and senior management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Participative (bottom-up) budget development does not yield information known to management but not to employees. Bottom-up budget development involves all the people affected by the budget, even lower-level employees, and it involves negotiation between lower-level managers and senior management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Participative (bottom-up) budget development does not minimize the cost of developing budgets. In fact, participative budgeting is more time-consuming than authoritative budgeting because lower-level managers and employees need to meet and negotiate their budgets, so the cost of participative learning may be greater. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-1.q: The goals and objectives upon which an annual profit plan is most effectively based are
o: A combination of financial, quantitative, and qualitative measures.
Financial measures such as net income, return on investment, and earnings per share.
Quantitative measures such as growth in unit sales, number of employees, and manufacturing capacity.
Qualitative measures of organizational activity such as product innovation leadership, product quality levels, and product safety.
3-1.e: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative and qualitative measures. The development of goals and objectives is the first step in the planning process. Top management must establish the major goals and objectives, set priorities and communicate these priorities to the people within the organization. Lower levels of the organization bear a part of the responsibility for the overall organizational goals and objectives, which become subunits' goals and objectives. However, specific departments' goals and objectives may conflict with other departments' goals and objectives. For example, a goal of an increase in market size may conflict with a goal of profitability of sales. Thus, profit plans should be based on multiple measures.

wrongWhy.1: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative, qualitative measures. This answer includes only financial measures.
wrongWhy.2: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative, qualitative measures. This answer includes only quantitative measures.
wrongWhy.3: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative, qualitative measures. This answer includes only qualitative measures.

3-1.q: The major objectives of budgeting are to
o: define responsibility centers, facilitate the identification of blame for missed budget predictions, and ensure goal congruence between superiors and subordinates.
foster the planning of operations, provide a framework for performance evaluation, and promote communication and coordination among the organization’s segments.
foster the planning of operations, facilitate the identification of blame for missed budget predictions, and ensure goal congruence between superiors and subordinates.
define responsibility centers, provide a framework for performance evaluation, and promote communication and coordination among the organization’s segments.
3-1.e: These are major objectives of budgeting.

wrongWhy.0: Defining responsibility centers and facilitating identification of blame for missed budget predictions are not objectives of budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: Facilitating the identification of blame for missed budget predictions is not one of the objectives of budgeting.
wrongWhy.3: Defining responsibility centers is not one of the objectives of budgeting.

3-1.q: The following sequence of steps are employed by a company to develop its annual profit plan. Planning guidelines are disseminated downward by top management after receiving input from all levels of management. A sales budget is prepared by individual sales units reflecting the sales targets of the various segments. This provides the basis for departmental production budgets and other related components by the various operating units. Communication is primarily lateral with some upward communication possible. A profit plan is submitted to top management for coordination and review. Top management's recommendations and revisions are acted upon by middle management. A revised profit plan is resubmitted for further review to top management. Top management grants final approval and distributes the formal plan downward to the various operating units. This outline of steps best describes which one of the following approaches to budget development?
o: Bottom-up approach.
Top-down approach.
Total justification of all activities by operating units.
Imposed budgeting by top management.
3-1.e: While senior management provides guidelines, the bulk of the budget is developed from input from individual departments. After submission to top management for review, top management's recommendations and revisions are made at the individual department level, and the individual department managers prepare and resubmit revised budget data to top management. This continues until the desired result is achieved for the consolidated budget.

wrongWhy.1: Management did not provide the budgeted numbers, just made recommendations for revisions to the budgets submitted by the various departments.
wrongWhy.2: This would describe zero based budgeting and is not described by the activities listed above. With zero based budgeting, every line item is analyzed and justified in a time consuming process. The guidelines developed from managerial input do not indicate such a thorough analysis.
wrongWhy.3: Management did not dictate the budget, just provided guidelines to give each department an idea of where the company should be headed.

3-1.q: Which one of the following best describes a reason why a company’s budgeting should be based on the company’s strategic plans?
o: Establishes standards to measure employee performance.
Helps control costs so that products can be sold profitably.
Identifies the external factors that have changed from the prior year and those that remain the same.
Identifies resources needed to reach strategic goals.
3-1.e: The process of developing the operating budgets for the individual units in an organization includes identifying the resources each unit will need to carry out the planned activities.

wrongWhy.0: Standard costs are used in budgeting. However, that is not a reason why a company’s budgeting should be based on the company’s strategic plans.
wrongWhy.1: A budget does provide the standard against which actual financial results are compared. However, that is not a reason why a company’s budgeting should be based on the company’s strategic plans.
wrongWhy.2: The strategic planning process does involve identifying external factors that affect the company. However, that is not a reason why a company’s budgeting should be based on the company’s strategic plans.

3-1.q: Suboptimal decision making is not likely to occur when:
o: The subunits in the organization compete with each other for the same input factors or for the same customers.
Guidance is given to subunit managers about how standards and goals affect them.
There is little congruence among the overall organization goals, the subunit goals, and the individual goals of decision makers.
Goals and standards of performance are set by the top-management.
3-1.e: This is correct. Guidance provided to managers allows them to work at their optimal level within the constraints.

wrongWhy.0: Creating a culture of competition within an organization for scarce resources (be they input factors or customers) cannot result in a win for the company as a whole. The company is directing their efforts inward rather than outward and opportunities will be missed.
wrongWhy.2: When goals at various levels are not connected there are many decisions that will be made with ineffective information and therefore the decisions will not necessarily be the best possible.
wrongWhy.3: Senior management is not fully aware of day to day operations and would not be in the best position to develop the most optimal goals and standards. They simply don't have the best knowledge of the situation.

3-1.q: Which one of the following is most important to a successful budgeting effort?
o: Experienced analysts
Top management support
Reliable forecasts and trend analysis
Integrated budget software
3-1.e: The support of top management is critical to gain the support of lower-level managers, and the support of lower-level managers is critical in order to gain the support of the affected employees. Without this support from above, the budget effort will be wasted because personnel will not take the process seriously.

wrongWhy.0: Experienced analysts are not most important to a successful budgeting effort.
wrongWhy.2: Reliable forecasts and trend analysis are not most important to a successful budgeting effort.
wrongWhy.3: Integrated budget software is not most mportant to a successful budgeting effort.

3-1.q: When budgets are used to evaluate performance and to set limits on spending, the process will often result in departments adding something "extra" to ensure the budgets will be met. This "extra" is
o: Strategic planning.
Continuous budgeting.
Budgetary slack.
Management by objectives.
3-1.e: When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either revenues are understated or expenses are overstated and this slack makes it difficult to properly evaluate performance.

wrongWhy.0: Long-term (or Strategic) planning is usually for periods of five years or longer and is based on the objectives of the organization. Some plans may extend up to 20 years. Strategic planning is directional, rather than operational. This means it focuses on where we want to go instead of specifically how we are going to get there.
wrongWhy.1: A continuous budget, also called a rolling budget, is one that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months.
wrongWhy.3: Management by objectives is a management approach that increases the amount of selfdirection that employees have. In MBO, a manager and his or her workers are responsible for agreeing upon their objectives and the methods to be used to obtain them.

3-1.q: All of the following are disadvantages of top-down budgeting as opposed to participatory budgeting, except that it:
o: May result in a budget that is not possible to achieve.
Reduces the time required for budgeting.
May limit the acceptance of proposed goals and objectives.
Reduces the communication between employees and management.
3-1.e: This is not a disadvantage of top-down budgeting. A top down budget does not require as much development time, as fewer voices must be heard.

wrongWhy.0: This is a disadvantage of top-down budgeting. Senior management may decree a desired outcome that is unrealistic under current working conditions. Senior management is not generally as familiar with day to day tasks and constraints as lower level managers and employees would be.
wrongWhy.2: This is a disadvantage of top-down budgeting. When senior management dictates the budget, lower level managers and their staff may not only not accept the budget, but they may also not accept senior management’s goals and objectives on which the budget is based. Senior management may very well be unaware of opportunities and strengths within their divisions. Often these opportunities are introduced by divisional management personnel who can see from the ground level how an opportunity can be capitalized upon. These leads are often unknown to senior management.
wrongWhy.3: This is a disadvantage of top-down budgeting. A participatory budget process provides management at all levels an opportunity to solicit ideas from employees. Without this approach, communication between employees and management is reduced, as employees may not feel as open to voicing opinions.

3-1.q: Budgetary slack can best be described as
o: The planned overestimation of budgeted expenses.
A plug number used to achieve a pre-set level of operating income.
The elimination of certain expenses to enhance budgeted income.
The planned underestimation of budgeted expenses.
3-1.e: When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists either revenues are understated or expenses are overstated to which makes it difficult to properly evaluate the performance.

wrongWhy.1: Budgetary slack is not a plug number.
wrongWhy.2: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. This is done in order to make it easy for the department, company or individual to meet the budget. The elimination of certain items is not budgetary slack, as it is rather making the budget appear better than the actual results will be.
wrongWhy.3: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated.

3-1.q: Budgeting problems where departmental managers are repeatedly achieving easy goals or failing to achieve demanding goals can be best minimized by establishing:
o: Preventive controls.
A policy that allows managers to build slack into the budget.
Better communication whereby managers discuss budget matters daily with their superiors.
Participative budgeting where managers pursue objectives consistent with those set by top management.
3-1.e: This is correct. By allowing managers to participate in the budgeting process with the objectives of senior management in mind, the managers can evaluate the department and find the most optimal, and realistic, means to achieve the targets.

wrongWhy.0: Enacting policies that "control" the budget process can result in resentment and ineffective efforts from departmental management. This is not the best way to generate a realistically challenging budget.
wrongWhy.1: Building slack into a budget is not going to be in the organization’s best interest in the long run.
wrongWhy.2: Budget discussions on a daily basis are a bit excessive and not an efficient way to conduct business. Generally there is a budget "season" where the development of the budget is the primary focus, and daily discussions are not unusual. After budget season; however, the communication between managers and their superiors would likely not focus solely on budget matters, although it would cover variances from the budget.

3-1.q: Which of the following best describes a controllable expense?
o: An expense that will remain semivariable in total over the relevant range in a given time period.
An expense whose actual amount will not normally differ from the standard (budget) amount.
An expected future expense that will be different under various alternatives.
An expense that is directly influenced at a given level of managerial authority within a given time period.
3-1.e: A controllable expense is an expense that is directly influenced at a given level of managerial authority within a given time period. Therefore, that manager is able to control this expense because he or she has the authority to make decisions about how the money will be spent.

wrongWhy.0: This is the definition of a semivariable cost.
wrongWhy.1: Any cost can differ from the standard (budget) amount and usually does.
wrongWhy.2: This is the definition of an incremental (differential) cost.

3-1.q: A company uses participative budgeting. In order to more easily meet budgetary goals, the controller underestimates the amount of revenue and overestimates fixed selling and administrative expenses. This is an example of
o: budgetary variance.
budgetary slack.
zero-based budgeting.
flexible budgeting.
3-1.e: This is a description of budgetary slack. Budgetary slack is the practice of underestimating planned revenues and overestimating planned costs to make the overall budgeted profit more achievable. Budgetary slack is the difference between the amount budgeted and the amount the manager actually expects.

wrongWhy.0: This is not a description of budgetary variance. In general, a budget variance is the difference between the actual results and the budgeted amount.
wrongWhy.2: This is not a description of zero-based budgeting. Under zero-based budgeting, the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period. Every planned activity must be justified with a cost-benefit analysis. In zero-based budgeting, all of the activities that a department undertakes are identified and then justified. Only revenues and costs from activities that are justified are included in the budget.
wrongWhy.3: This is not a description of flexible budgeting. A flexible budget is a budget that is prepared using the budgeted revenues and costs per unit and the actual level of activity (sales or production, as appropriate). It is essentially what the budget would have been if the company had known what the actual level of sales or production would be in advance and had used that information when it developed the budget.

3-1.q: Each organization plans and budgets its operations for slightly different reasons. Which one of the following is not a significant reason for planning and budgeting?
o: Providing a basis for controlling operations.
Forcing managers to consider expected future trends and conditions.
Checking progress toward the objectives of the organization.
Ensuring profitable operations.
3-1.e: The budget is a realistic plan expressed in quantitative terms. A budget serves as a number of tools: planning, control, motivation and communication tools. However, the budget by itself is not able to ensure profitable operations. "Ensure" means guarantee. A budget, if it is used properly to control operations, can help to increase the probability that operations will be profitable. But it cannot ensure that they will be profitable.

wrongWhy.0: Providing a basis for controlling operations is a reason for developing plans and budgets.
wrongWhy.1: Forcing managers to consider expected future trends and conditions is a reason for developing plans and budgets.
wrongWhy.2: Checking progress toward the objectives of the organization is a reason for developing plans and budgets.

3-1.q: The first step in developing a master budget is to prepare the
o: selling and administrative budget.
cash budget.
sales budget.
production budget.
3-1.e: The sales budget is the first budget to be prepared when developing the master budget.

wrongWhy.0: The selling and administrative budget is not the first budget to be prepared when developing the master budget.
wrongWhy.1: The cash budget is the last budget to be prepared when developing the master budget.
wrongWhy.3: The production budget is not the first budget to be prepared when developing the master budget.

3-1.q: All of the following are criticisms of the traditional budgeting process except that it
o: incorporates non-financial measures as well as financial measures into its output.
is not used until the end of the budget period to evaluate performance.
overemphasizes a fixed time horizon such as one year.
makes across-the-board cuts when early budget iterations show that planned expenses are too high.
3-1.e: This is not a criticism of the traditional budgeting process or of any budgeting process. A budget needs to incorporate non-financial measures into its output. For example, expectations regarding units to be manufactured and sold in the coming period, number of employees that will be needed, and new products to be introduced, though non-financial measures, will affect the financial items in the budget.

wrongWhy.1: This is a criticism of traditional budgeting, because this can occur. Actual performance for a period is measured against budgeted performance for that period. While it is necessary to wait until the end of a period to measure the budgetary variance for the whole period, the budget period can be broken into smaller timeframes. A 12 month budget can, and often will, be divided into monthly amounts to allow for current month and year-to-date budget variance reporting throughout the year, so that operational adjustments can be made as necessary.
wrongWhy.2: This is a criticism of traditional budgeting. Those who criticize it question why a budget needs to have a fixed time horizon such as one year. They say that different segments of the same company have different needs for planning. The purpose of a forecast should be to recognize issues that need to be planned for in time to plan for them, and this will be different for every area of the company. So different forecasting horizons are needed for different areas, and they need to be monitored individually. One size does not fit all in an organization today.
wrongWhy.3: This is a criticism of traditional budgeting. The traditional budgeting process may lead to across-theboard cuts when early budget iterations show that planned expenses are too high. However, budgeting should not necessarily require across the board cuts, even when expenses are higher than desired. Cost cutting should be based on what is best for the organization. Frequently cuts based on what is best for the organization will not be equally distributed.

3-1.q: An exercise equipment manufacturer uses the variable costing method and has determined that the total manufacturing costs of its single 30-pound barbell is $27 per unit. The direct material cost per unit is $16. The allocated administration expenses are $6 per unit. The allocated fixed rental costs are $3 per unit. If no other costs are incurred, the direct labor cost per unit is
3-1.e: Under vriable costing, the fixed rental costs are expensed in the period that they are incurred and not allocated to the units produced. Also, the administrtaive costs are not included in the manufacturing costs. Since the total manufacturing costs are $27 per unit and we know that the direct material osts are $16 per unit, there is a remaining $11 of manufacturing costs that will be either variable overheads or direct labor. We are told in the question that no additional costs were incurred other than direct materials, so the direct material cost is $11 per unit.

wrongWhy.1: This answer incorrectly treats the administration expenses as a manufacturing costs under variable costing. Administration costs are not manufacturing costs.
wrongWhy.2: This answer incorrectly treats the fixed rent as part of the manufacturing costs under variable costing. Under vriable costing, the fixed rental costs are expensed in the period that they are incurred and not allocated to the units produced. Also, this answer incorrectly treats the administration expenses as a manufacturing costs under variable costing. Administration costs are not manufacturing costs.
wrongWhy.3: This answer incorrectly treats the fixed rent as part of the manufacturing costs under variable costing. Under vriable costing, the fixed rental costs are expensed in the period that they are incurred and not allocated to the units produced.

3-1.q: All of the following are advantages of the use of budgets in a management control system except that budgets:
o: Promote communication and coordination within the organization.
Limit unauthorized expenditures.
Provide performance criteria.
Force management planning.
3-1.e: Even the most advanced and accurate budget cannot prevent unauthorized expenditures. This is controlled through management oversight, rather than the budgeting process.

wrongWhy.0: Throughout the budget process the various departments within an organization will meet to plan for the next year. These meetings will foster cooperation, communication and familiarity which will carry over after budgeting season.
wrongWhy.2: Budgets provide performance information, in that management can analyze what they thought would happen vs. what actually happened. From that point they can determine what factors were within their control and what was outside of their control and use this information for future budgets and operational decisions.
wrongWhy.3: Forcing management to focus on the future and make appropriate plans for the organization's success is a significant advantage of the use of budgets.

3-1.q: Which of the following types of budget systems most strongly supports the objective of improving communication and promoting coordination?
o: Top down, fixed budgets.
Bottom up, fixed budgets.
Bottom up, flexible budgets.
Top down, flexible budgets.
3-1.e: Bottom up budgeting and flexible budgeting both will improve communication and promote coordination.

wrongWhy.0: Fixed budgets do not support the improvement of communication, and top down budgeting does not promote coordination.
wrongWhy.1: Fixed budgets do not support the improvement of communication because once the budget has been completed, the communication process largely stops.
wrongWhy.3: Top down budgeting does not promote coordination.

3-1.q: The budgeting process should be one that motivates managers and employees to work toward organizational goals. Which one of the following is least likely to motivate managers?
o: Participation by subordinates in the budgetary process.
Use of management by exception.
Holding subordinates accountable for the items they control.
Having top management set budget levels.
3-1.e: A budget is a very useful tool in a number of areas: planning, control, evaluation, motivation, communication, identifying future problems. However, when top management sets the budget without any participation from those who will be responsible for meeting the budgeted goals, employees will not have a sense of ownership of the plan. This will not be motivating and thus, not very effective.

wrongWhy.0: Participation in the budgetary process would motivate managers by giving them a sense of ownership of the budget.
wrongWhy.1: Management by exception lets managers concentrate their attention on areas where problems are. This will likely be a motivating item for managers as they will feel that their efforts are being directed where they are needed.
wrongWhy.2: Holding subordinates accountable for the items they control would most likely motivate managers and encourage a better performance.

3-1.q: Budgets are a necessary component of financial decision making because they help provide a(n)
o: means to check managerial discretion.
automatic corrective mechanism for errors.
efficient allocation of resources.
means to use all the firm's resources.
3-1.e: A budget is a realistic plan for the future expressed in quantitative terms. A budget serves as a planning, control, and evaluation tool. As such, the use of a budget helps to allocate resources efficiently.

wrongWhy.0: Budgets are not used check managerial discretion.
wrongWhy.1: Budgets do not provide an automatic corrective mechanism for errors.
wrongWhy.3: Budgets help to provide effective and efficient use of resources, not just the basic usage use of all resources.

3-1.q: Which one of the following best describes the role of top management in the budgeting process? Top management
o: needs to be involved, including using the budget process to communicate goals.
lacks the detailed knowledge of the daily operations and should limit their involvement.
needs to separate the budgeting process and the business planning process into two separate processes.
should be involved only in the approval process.
3-1.e: The budget is a very useful tool and can serve as a tool in a number of areas: planning, control, evaluation, motivation, communication, identifying future problems. To use the budget as a communication and motivational tool top management should be involved in budgeting process.

wrongWhy.1: The budget is a very useful tool and can serve as a tool in a number of areas: planning, control, evaluation, motivation, communication, identifying future problems. To use the budget as a communication and motivational tool, top management should be involved in budgeting process even though they lack detailed knowledge of the daily operations.
wrongWhy.2: The budget is a realistic plan for the future expressed in quantitative terms. In other words, budgeting is a part of overall planning process.
wrongWhy.3: The budget is a very useful tool and can serve as a tool in a number of areas: planning, control, evaluation, motivation, communication, identifying future problems. To use the budget as a communication and motivation tool, the top management should be involved in the whole budgeting process, not only in the approval process.

3-1.q: A company pays its production manager an annual bonus based on how well the manager performs against the production department’s annual budgets. The production manager has been overestimating budgeted costs the past few years in order to obtain a higher bonus payment. The production manager’s actions are best described as
o: balancing production costs.
building budgetary slack.
setting budgeted performance.
motivating employee effort.
3-1.e: Budgetary slack is the difference between the amount budgeted and the amount the manager actually expects. Building in budgetary slack is the practice of underestimating planned revenues and overestimating planned costs to make the overall budgeted profit more achievable. This is what the production manager is doing.

wrongWhy.0: This is not the behavior that the manager is exhibiting.
wrongWhy.2: This is not the behavior that the manager is exhibiting.
wrongWhy.3: This is not the behavior that the manager is exhibiting.

3-1.q: Which one of the following reasons is not a significant reason for planning in an organization?
o: Monitoring profitable operations.
Forcing managers to consider expected future trends and conditions.
Developing a basis for controlling operations.
Promoting coordination among operating units.
3-1.e: Monitoring is a control function, and although planning is necessary in order to control an operation, planning is not done in order to monitor only the profitable operations. All operations should be monitored. For that reason, this is the least significant reason for planning from among the answer choices given.

wrongWhy.1: In the planning process managers need to consider future trends and conditions and this will assist the company in their planning for, and potentially avoiding, negative events in the future.
wrongWhy.2: Without a plan it is very difficult to control, so this is a reason for planning.
wrongWhy.3: The coordination of efforts between operating units is a reason for planning because planning helps make certain that everyone is working towards the same goal.

3-1.q: Which one of the following statements concerning approaches for the budget development process is correct?
o: To prevent ambiguity, once departmental budgeted goals have been developed, they should remain fixed even if the sales forecast upon which they are based proves to be wrong in the middle of the fiscal year.
Since department managers have the most detailed knowledge about organizational operations, they should use this information as the building blocks of the operating budget.
The top-down approach to budgeting will not ensure adherence to strategic organizational goals.
With the information technology available, the role of budgets as an organizational communication device has declined.
3-1.e: This is correct. It is the department manager who is best aware of day to day operations, local issues that affect the department, opportunities for efficiencies, and obstacles to success. These factors will assist in developing a budget that includes challenging and realistic goals that are accepted by the manager and the employees.

wrongWhy.0: This statement is not correct. Budgets should always have some degree of flexibility. If forecasts are determined to be incorrect, then the budget committee should explore options to get the budget back on track in total, or to implement a contingency budget so that departments are still working with a realistically challenging goal in mind.
wrongWhy.2: This statement is not correct. Under the top down approach the budget will be developed specifically to achieve strategic organizational goals as that is the primary focus of senior management – overall organizational performance.
wrongWhy.3: This statement is not correct. Technology can certainly cut down on the time required for budgeting, but it cannot replace discussion among and between various departments and levels of management. The budget is a consistent reminder of the importance of having each department work toward an individual goal in order to meet the overall corporate objectives.

3-1.q: Which one of the following is usually not cited as being an advantage of a formal budgetary process?
o: Forces management to evaluate the reasonableness of assumptions used and goals identified in the budgetary process.
Ensures improved cost control within the organization and prevents inefficiencies.
Serves as a coordination and communication device between management and subordinates.
Provides a formal benchmark to be used for feedback and performance evaluation.
3-1.e: A budget is a realistic plan for the future expressed in quantitative terms. A budget is a very useful tool and can serve as a tool in a number of areas: planning, control, evaluation, motivation, communication, identifying future problems. Developing a budget is an important step in the process of control. However, this question is about having a formal budgetary process, not about having a budget. Furthermore, neither a budget nor a formal budgetary process will ensure improved cost control or prevent inefficiencies. After the budget has been developed, it must be used as a tool by management to improve the probability of achieving those objectives. However, even its use cannot ensure that those objectives will be achieved.

wrongWhy.0: One of the advantages of a budget is that it forces management to evaluate the reasonableness of assumptions used in the budgetary process and goals that have been set.
wrongWhy.2: The budget communicates and authorizes actions and also promotes coordination within an organization. This is an advantage of a budget.
wrongWhy.3: A budget provides criteria against which to measure performance during the upcoming period. Therefore, it does provide a formal benchmark to be used for feedback and performance evaluation, and that is an advantage of budgeting.

3-1.q: Which one of the following is not an advantage of a participatory budgeting process?
o: Goal congruence.
Communication between departments.
Coordination between departments.
Control of uncertainties.
3-1.e: Under any budgeting process, there will always be uncertainties that cannot be estimated or controlled, and this will be true with a participatory budgeting process as much as with any other type of budgeting process.

wrongWhy.0: This is an advantage of participatory budgeting.
wrongWhy.1: This is an advantage of participatory budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is an advantage of participatory budgeting.

3-1.q: The budgeting technique that is most likely to motivate managers is
o: zero-base budgeting.
top-down budgeting.
bottom-up budgeting.
program budgeting and review technique.
3-1.e: Bottom-up budgeting is the budgeting technique that motivates lower-level managers the most. The budgeting process starts with departments at the lowest level in the organization. Managers and subordinates set goals and objectives and translate those goals and objectives into quantitative budgets for their responsibility centers. Then the budgets are sent on to the next highest level, which does the same thing, and so forth. At the top the company's budget is developed by consolidating all of the lower-level budgets. Of course, top management reviews the lower-level budgets as well as the consolidated budget that results from them. If the company's consolidated budgeted results are not what top management wants to see, top management may negotiate changes to the lower-level budgets as necessary to achieve the company's objectives. Employees are more likely to support budgets when they have participated in their preparation. It gives them a feeling of ownership of the process and they will be more likely to support the budget's implementation.

wrongWhy.0: Zero-based budgeting is a budget which is prepared ignoring the past periods. The budget is developed from "zero" and all expenses need to be justified for the current period. The use of this method by itself is not going to motivate or demotivate managers.
wrongWhy.1: Top-down budgeting is less motivating for managers as according to this budgeting technique the plan is set by top-management and lower level managers do not participate in preparing it.
wrongWhy.3: Program budgets are formulated by objective rather than function. This type of budget by itself will not motivate or demotivate managers.

3-1.q: All of the following are disadvantages of authoritative budgeting as opposed to participatory budgeting, except that it
o: may limit the acceptance of proposed goals and objectives.
reduces the time required for budgeting.
reduces the communication between employees and management.
may result in a budget that is not possible to achieve.
3-1.e: Authoritative budgeting is budgeting from the "top down." Senior management sets the budget and presents it to mid-level managers as their targets to achieve. Mid-level managers have no input into the development of the budget. This does reduce the time required for budgeting, because communication between senior management and mid-level management over budget targets is eliminated. Although a reduction in time is not a disadvantage of authoritative budgeting, the advantage of reduced time has costs. Those costs are: (1) Without input from lower level managers, the budget developed by senior management may result in an impractical budget because it does not take into account any existing limitations that senior management may not be aware of. (2) It reduces communication between employees and managers and between middle managers and senior management, since input from mid-level managers is neither sought nor desired by senior management. (3) Without any input into the budgeting process, lower level managers and employees will not see the budget as their own. This may result in a lack of their acceptance of senior management's proposed goals and objectives.

wrongWhy.0: This is a true statement. An authoritative budget is one that is developed by senior management with no input from lower level employees and managers. Without any input into the budgeting process, lower level managers and employees will not see the budget as their own. This may result in a lack of their acceptance of senior management's proposed goals and objectives.
wrongWhy.2: This is a true statement. An authoritative budget is one that is developed by senior management with no input from lower level employees and managers. It reduces communication between employees and managers and between middle managers and senior management, since input from mid-level managers is neither sought nor desired by senior management.
wrongWhy.3: This is a true statement. An authoritative budget is one that is developed by senior management with no input from lower level employees and managers. Without input from lower level employees and managers, a budget may result in an impractical budget that is not achievable, because it does not take into account existing limitations that senior management may not be aware of.

3-1.q: To meet budgetary goals more easily, a company controller underestimates the amount of revenue and overestimates fixed selling and administrative expenses during the budgeting process. This is an example of
o: budgetary slack.
budgetary variance.
zero-based budgeting.
flexible budgeting.
3-1.e: This is the definition of budgetary slack.

wrongWhy.1: This is not what a budgetary variance is.
wrongWhy.2: This is not what zero-based budgeting is.
wrongWhy.3: This is not what flexible budgeting is.

3-1.q: Which one of the following is not considered to be a benefit of participative budgeting?
o: When managers set the final targets for the budget, top management need not be concerned with the overall profitability of current operations.
Individuals at all organizational levels are recognized as being part of the team; this results in greater support of the organization.
Managers are more motivated to reach the budget goals since they participated in setting them.
The budget estimates are prepared by those in direct contact with various activities.
3-1.e: Just because managers that are more directly involved with the activity have participated in the budgeting process, does not mean that top management does not need to be concerned about overall profitability of current operations.

wrongWhy.1: This is a benefit of participatory budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is a benefit of participatory budgeting.
wrongWhy.3: This is a benefit of participatory budgeting.

3-1.q: Diana Stinson, Cherry Valley Inc.'s factory manager, had lost her patience. Six months ago, she had appointed a team from the production and service departments to finalize the allocation of costs and setting of standard costs. They were still feuding, and so she had hired Brennan and Rose, a large consulting firm, to resolve the matter. All of the following are potential consequences of having the standards set by Brennan and Rose except that
o: Employees could react negatively since they did not participate in setting the standards.
The standards may appear to lack management support.
Brennan and Rose may not fully understand Cherry Valley's manufacturing process, resulting in suboptimal performance.
There could be dissatisfaction if the standards contain costs which are not controllable by the unit held responsible.
3-1.e: If management has hired an outside company to develop standards, the standards will appear to have management support, since management has delegated the responsibility to the outside company. Therefore, the appearance of lacking management support is not a potential consequence of having the standards set by an outside company.

wrongWhy.0: Employees are likely to feel left out as the consultants arrive and therefore may not accept the consultants' recommendations. They may not be motivated to meet the standards.
wrongWhy.2: As outside consultants they cannot be as immediately familiar with the corporate manufacturing processes as internal teams would be, and the resulting standards may be unworkable.
wrongWhy.3: If a company segment is being held responsible for costs, that segment needs to have the authority to control those costs. If standards are set by an outside organization, that organization most likely will not know what costs are controlled by what company segments. Therefore, the standards could contain costs that are not controllable by the unit being held responsible, which could lead to de-motivation on the part of employees.

3-1.q: One of the primary advantages of budgeting is that it
o: bases the profit plan on estimates.
is continually adapted to fit changing circumstances.
does not take the place of management and administration.
requires departmental managers to make plans in conjunction with the plans of other interdependent departments.
3-1.e: The budget is a realistic plan for the future expressed in quantitative terms. The budget serves as planning, control, coordination, evaluation tool, etc. Budgets of individual departments are formed into one organizational budget. Thus, one of the primary advantages of budgeting is that it requires departmental managers to make plans in conjunction with the plans of other interdependent departments, thus promoting coordination and communication among organization units and activities.

wrongWhy.0: It is true that the profit plan is based on estimates. However, this is neither an advantage nor a disadvantage of budgeting.
wrongWhy.1: It is true that the budget should be adapted to fit changing circumstances (though not continually -- only when needed). However, this is neither an advantage nor a disadvantage of budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: It is true that budgeting does not take the place of management and administration. However, this is neither an advantage nor a disadvantage of budgeting.

3-1.q:
Given the following data for Scurry Company, what is the cost of goods sold?
Beginning inventory of finished goods: | $100,000
Cost of goods manufactured: | 700,000
Ending inventory of finished goods: | 200,000
Beginning work-in-process inventory: | 300,000
Ending work-in-process inventory: | 50,000
3-1.e: Cost of goods sold is beginning finished goods inventory + cost of goods manufactured/purchases − ending finished goods inventory. So cost of goods sold is $100,000 + $700,000 − $200,000 = $600,000.

wrongWhy.0: This is cost of goods manufactured minus ending inventory of finished goods. Beginning inventory of finished goods needs to be accounted for, as well.
wrongWhy.1: This is cost of goods manufactured plus beginning inventory of finished goods. Ending inventory of finished goods needs to be accounted for, as well.
wrongWhy.3: This is cost of goods manufactured adjusted by the amount of change in work-in-process inventory. Cost of goods sold is cost of goods manufactured adjusted by the amount of change in finished goods inventory.

3-1.q: One approach for developing standard costs incorporates communication, bargaining, and interaction among product line managers; the immediate supervisors for whom the standards are being developed; and the accountants and engineers before the standards are accepted by top management. This approach would best be characterized as a(n):
o: Centralized top-down approach.
Imposed approach.
Team development approach.
Engineering approach.
3-1.e: Communication, bargaining, and interaction involve multiple levels and multiple departments in the development of standards. This makes it more probable that every aspect of the final standards have been well thought out and reviewed by all parties concerned. This characterizes a team development approach.

wrongWhy.0: This is incorrect as there exists a considerable amount of communication and negotiation before senior management accepts the standards in this situation.
wrongWhy.1: This implies that information is dictated. With four levels of involvement before top management accepts a number, the approach described is more participative.
wrongWhy.3: An engineering approach focuses on the technical standards required for production. Here we also have input from the accountants and line managers as well, making it a little more all encompassing than a traditional engineering approach.

3-1.q: From the perspective of corporate management, the use of budgetary slack
o: Increases the effectiveness of the corporate planning process.
Increases the ability to identify potential budget weaknesses.
Increases the probability that budgets will not be achieved.
Increases the likelihood of inefficient resource allocation.
3-1.e: The use of budgetary slack increases the likelihood of inefficient resource allocation. When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated, or both. Hence, management won't work hard on increasing revenues or minimizing costs as they simply have to achieve easily attainable goals. Thus, the resources most likely will be allocated inefficiently.

wrongWhy.0: The use of budgetary slack does not increase the effectiveness of the corporate planning process. Budgetary slack exists when budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated, or both. Budgets should set high but with attainable performance standards. However, budgetary slack makes it easy to achieve the budgeted level of performance. Thus, the effectiveness of the corporate planning process decreases when there is budgetary slack.
wrongWhy.1: The use of budgetary slack does not increase the ability to identify potential budget weaknesses. Budgetary slack exists when budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated. Since the budgetary slack distorts a real attainable level of performance it is difficult to identify potential budget weaknesses.
wrongWhy.2: The use of budgetary slack does not increase the probability that budgets will not be achieved--just the opposite. When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated, or both. Thus, budgetary slack increases the probability that budgets will be achieved.

3-1.q: The manager of a responsibility center that is accountable for costs incurred but not for revenue earned or for the capital invested in the center is the manager of
o: A cost center.
A profit center.
An investment center.
A revenue center.
3-1.e: A cost center is a responsibility center whose manager is held accountable only for the costs incurred in the center. A maintenance department, the accounting department and a production department are typical cost centers: they consume resources but do not generate revenue and do not control the capital invested in them.

wrongWhy.1: A profit center manager is accountable for both costs and revenues, and so is evaluated on the profit the center generates. That is a wider responsibility than costs alone.
wrongWhy.2: An investment center manager is accountable for costs, revenues and the capital invested in the center, and is usually evaluated on a return measure such as return on investment.
wrongWhy.3: A revenue center manager is accountable for revenue generated, not for the costs of producing what is sold.

3-1.q: Which one of the following costs is most likely to be controllable by the supervisor of a production department?
o: Depreciation on the factory building.
Indirect materials consumed in the supervisor's department.
The department's allocated share of corporate headquarters salaries.
Property taxes on the plant.
3-1.e: A cost is controllable at a given level of management if the manager at that level can significantly influence the amount of the cost through his or her own decisions. The quantity of indirect materials such as glue, screws and cleaning supplies consumed in the department is driven by day-to-day decisions the supervisor makes, so it is controllable by that supervisor.

wrongWhy.0: Depreciation on the building is a committed fixed cost that results from a capital decision made long before, usually by senior management. The department supervisor cannot influence it.
wrongWhy.2: An allocated share of headquarters salaries is assigned to the department by a formula. The supervisor has no influence over either the size of those salaries or the allocation method.
wrongWhy.3: Property taxes are set by the taxing authority on the assessed value of the plant. No decision the supervisor makes changes the amount.

3-1.q: Variances between actual and budgeted costs for a responsibility center should be reported by
o: The accounting department, because it maintains the accounting records.
The person who makes the decisions that affect the cost.
Senior management, so that the reporting remains objective.
An external consultant, so that departmental bias is removed.
3-1.e: The person responsible for making the decisions that affect a cost should also be the person who reports on the variances between actual and planned amounts. That person budgeted for the cost, makes the spending decisions, and is best placed to make whatever operational adjustments the variance shows are needed.

wrongWhy.0: The accounting department compiles and distributes the reports, but it does not make the spending decisions and cannot explain why a variance arose or what should be done about it.
wrongWhy.2: Senior management reviews variance reports; it does not prepare them. Senior managers are too far from the daily decisions to identify causes or corrective actions.
wrongWhy.3: An outside consultant has no knowledge of the operating decisions behind the numbers and no authority to make the operational adjustments the variance calls for.

3-1.q: A division manager is evaluated on the division's total reported costs, which include an allocated share of corporate advertising that the manager cannot influence. The most likely consequence is that
o: The manager will find ways to reduce corporate advertising spending.
The manager will regard the evaluation as unfair, and motivation will suffer.
Goal congruence between the division and the company will improve.
The division's controllable costs will fall.
3-1.e: Managers should be evaluated only on the costs they can control. When non-controllable allocated costs are included in the performance measure, the manager is being held responsible for an outcome he or she cannot influence. The predictable result is that the evaluation is seen as unfair and motivation falls.

wrongWhy.0: Corporate advertising is decided at the corporate level. A division manager has no authority over it, which is precisely why including it in the evaluation is a problem.
wrongWhy.2: Goal congruence improves when performance measures line up with what the manager actually controls. Including non-controllable costs works against goal congruence, not for it.
wrongWhy.3: Nothing about the allocation changes the costs the manager does control. It simply adds an uncontrollable amount on top of them.

3-1.q: Standards that can be met only under perfect operating conditions, with no machine breakdowns, no waste and no employee downtime, will most likely
o: Motivate employees to their maximum level of effort.
Discourage employees, because the standard is regarded as unattainable.
Produce favorable variances in most periods.
Permanently eliminate budgetary slack from the budget.
3-1.e: A standard motivates only if the person held to it believes it can be reached. An ideal standard that assumes perfect conditions will be missed in virtually every period, so employees stop treating it as a goal and the resulting variances stop carrying information. Standards should be set at a level that is high but attainable.

wrongWhy.0: The opposite happens. Once a target is seen as impossible, effort falls rather than rises, because additional effort makes no difference to whether the standard is met.
wrongWhy.2: Ideal standards allow for no inefficiency at all, so actual results will almost always be worse than standard. The variances produced will be persistently unfavorable, not favorable.
wrongWhy.3: The problem with an ideal standard is the opposite of slack. Slack arises when standards are too easy; an ideal standard is too hard.

3-1.q: A company engages an outside consulting firm to set the standard costs for its production departments. Compared with standards developed by the departments themselves, the most likely consequence is
o: Greater acceptance of the standards by the people held to them.
Lower acceptance, because those held to the standards had no part in setting them.
Standards that reflect day-to-day operating conditions more accurately.
Faster achievement of goal congruence throughout the company.
3-1.e: Outside consultants bring objectivity and can settle internal disputes quickly, but they leave no ownership behind. People who had no voice in setting a standard are less likely to accept it or to feel committed to meeting it, which is the central behavioural drawback of imposing standards from outside.

wrongWhy.0: Acceptance comes from participation. Removing the departments from the process removes the main source of their commitment to the resulting standards.
wrongWhy.2: Consultants lack the detailed operating knowledge that departmental staff have. Standards set from outside are more likely, not less likely, to miss the practical realities of the work.
wrongWhy.3: Goal congruence is built by involving the people whose behaviour the budget is meant to guide. Imposing standards from outside does not achieve it faster.

3-1.q: For a standard to work as both a planning tool and a motivational tool, it should be set at a level that is
o: Easily achieved by every employee.
Achievable only under ideal conditions.
High but attainable.
Equal to the previous period's actual results.
3-1.e: A standard set high but attainable stretches performance while remaining credible. It gives a realistic basis for planning the budget and, because employees believe it can be met, it also motivates the effort needed to meet it.

wrongWhy.0: A standard that everyone meets easily contains slack. Actual results will beat it routinely, so it neither stretches performance nor provides a meaningful benchmark for control.
wrongWhy.1: A standard achievable only under ideal conditions is missed in every period, so it stops functioning as a goal and its variances stop being informative.
wrongWhy.3: Last period's actual results include whatever inefficiency occurred in that period. Building those inefficiencies into the standard locks them in permanently.

3-1.q: Which one of the following is most essential to a successful budgeting process?
o: Preparing the entire budget within the accounting department.
Active support and participation from top management.
Setting goals that are certain to be achieved.
Keeping the completed budget confidential from operating managers.
3-1.e: Without visible support from top management, a budget is treated as an accounting exercise rather than as the company's operating plan. Top management sets the goals, establishes the priorities and provides the resources that make the process work, and its participation signals to everyone else that the budget matters.

wrongWhy.0: Accounting coordinates and compiles the budget, but operating managers hold the knowledge of what is achievable and what resources are needed. A budget built in isolation by accounting will be neither accurate nor accepted.
wrongWhy.2: Goals that are certain to be achieved contain budgetary slack. They remove the motivational value of the budget and weaken it as a control tool.
wrongWhy.3: The budget is the operating plan for the managers who must carry it out. Withholding it from them defeats its purpose as a planning, coordinating and communication tool.

3-1.q: A company's budget guidelines are vague, submission deadlines are unclear, and operating managers receive the approved budget only after the budget year has begun. The budgeting process most clearly lacks
o: Budgetary slack.
Clear communication of budget guidelines and an enforced timetable.
A flexible budget.
A capital expenditures budget.
3-1.e: A successful budgeting process needs clearly communicated guidelines, defined responsibilities and a timetable that is actually met, so that the budget is complete and in the hands of the managers before the period it covers begins. A budget delivered after the year starts cannot guide the decisions made in that year.

wrongWhy.0: Budgetary slack is a distortion built deliberately into budget figures. Its absence is desirable, and nothing in the situation described points to slack.
wrongWhy.2: A flexible budget is a tool for evaluating performance after the fact by restating the budget at actual volume. It does not address guidelines, deadlines or communication.
wrongWhy.3: A capital expenditures budget covers long-term asset purchases and is prepared years in advance. It is unrelated to the breakdown in process discipline described here.

3-1.q: Which of the following correctly describes the time frames of a company's long-range strategic plan compared with its annual profit plan?
o: The strategic plan covers one year, and the annual profit plan covers three to five years.
The strategic plan covers three to five years or longer, and the annual profit plan covers one year, usually broken into months or quarters.
Both cover exactly one year, but the strategic plan is prepared only by senior management.
The strategic plan covers a single operating cycle, and the annual profit plan covers a single month.
3-1.e: The strategic (long-range) plan sets out the company's goals and direction over an extended horizon, commonly three to five years or more, and is stated largely in qualitative and broad quantitative terms. The annual profit plan, or budget, translates the first year of that direction into detailed quantitative terms and is normally subdivided into months or quarters so that actual results can be compared against it as the year unfolds.

wrongWhy.0: This reverses the two horizons. The detailed, quantified plan is the one-year annual profit plan; the multi-year plan is the strategic plan.
wrongWhy.2: The two plans do not cover the same period. The distinguishing feature is the length of the horizon and the level of detail, not who prepares them.
wrongWhy.3: Neither horizon is described correctly. A strategic plan spans several years, and an annual profit plan covers a full year, not a single month, although it is broken down into shorter interim periods.

3-1.q: Which of the following statements about the time frames used in budgeting is correct?
o: A short-term budget is usually prepared for a period of one year or less and is generally broken down into shorter interim periods such as months or quarters.
A long-term budget covers no more than 18 months, because forecasts beyond that period are unreliable.
A company's budget time frame must be identical for every department within the company.
Short-term budgets are prepared only by companies that have no long-range plan.
3-1.e: Budgets are prepared for different time frames depending on their purpose. The short-term budget, which is the annual profit plan, typically covers one year or less and is subdivided into months or quarters so that performance can be monitored and corrective action taken during the period rather than only at year end.

wrongWhy.1: There is no 18-month ceiling. Long-term budgets and plans routinely extend three to five years or longer, and capital expenditure budgets in particular may cover much longer horizons.
wrongWhy.2: Different departments and different types of budget legitimately use different horizons. A capital expenditures budget, for example, commonly spans several years while an operating department's budget covers one year.
wrongWhy.3: Short-term and long-term budgets are complements, not alternatives. A company with a long-range plan still prepares an annual profit plan, and in fact the annual plan should be derived from the long-range plan.

3-2.q:
Barnes Corporation expected to sell 150,000 board games during the month of November, and the company's master budget contained the following data related to the sale and production of these games:
Revenue: | $2,400,000
Direct materials: | 675,000
Direct labor: | 300,000
Variable overhead: | 450,000
Contribution: | $975,000
Fixed overhead: | 250,000
Fixed selling/administration: | 500,000
Operating income: | $225,000
Actual sales during November were 180,000 games. Using a flexible budget, the company expects the operating income for the month of November to be
3-2.e: The flexible budget is the budget developed for the actual achieved level of activity rather than the master budget level. To compute the flexible budget we must use the standard costing system, i.e. we need to determine the budgeted selling price, budgeted variable cost per unit, and budgeted total amount of fixed costs. The budgeted selling price minus the budgeted cost per unit equal the contribution per unit, so we can use the budgeted contribution per unit and need not calculate the first two items. The budgeted contribution per unit is $6.50 ($975,000 ÷ 150,000). Fixed costs for the flexible budget are the same as for the static budget, since fixed costs do not fluctuate with the level of output. The flexible budget contribution margin for sales of 180,000 is $1,170,000 ($6.50 × 180,000). Subtracting the fixed costs from the expected contribution margin for the actual level of sales, we arrive at a flexible budget operating income of $420,000 ($1,170,000 − $250,000 − $500,000 = $420,000).

wrongWhy.1: This answer results from incorrectly treating variable overhead costs as fixed costs. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.2: This is the static budget operating income not adjusted for the actual level of output.
wrongWhy.3: This is calculated using the static budget operating income per unit of $1.50 ($225,000 ÷ 150,000) and multiplied by the actual level of sales of 180,000. This gives the operating income of $270,000 ($1.5 × 180,000), but it ignores the fact that fixed costs do not vary with the level of activity.

3-2.q: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is a(n)
o: operating budget.
flexible budget.
life-cycle budget.
continuous budget.
3-2.e: A flexible budget takes the variable revenues and costs as they are planned in the master budget (the static budget) and adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity level had been used in preparing the budget. Thus, a flexible budget is developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period.

wrongWhy.0: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not an operating budget. Operating budgets are used to identify the resources that will be needed to carry out the planned activities during the budget period, such as sales, services, production, purchasing, marketing, and R&D (research and development). The operating budgets for individual units are compiled into the budgeted income statement. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not a life-cycle budget. A life-cycle budget is used to plan incomes and expenses for one specific product throughout its entire life cycle, from its development through its decline, enabling the company to see the cash flows that will result from the product over its entire life. When all the lifetime development and production costs are set forth in the life-cycle budget, management can set a price that will cover not only the company’s costs but also its required return on investment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not a continuous budget. A continuous budget, also called a rolling budget, is one that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous budget would be prepared at the end of every month or possibly quarter for the next twelve months. Each month or quarter, the month or quarter just completed is dropped from the budget and a new monthly or quarterly budget is added to the end of the budget. At the same time as a month or quarter is dropped and a new month or quarter is added, the other periods in the budget can be revised to reflect any new information that has become available. Thus, the budget is continuously being updated and always covers the same amount of time in the future. For example, in September 20X3, a monthly rolling budget will cover the months of October 20X3 through September 20X4. In October 20X3, the rolling budget will cover the months of November 20X3 through October 20X4. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: A budgeting approach that requires a manager to justify the entire budget for each budget period is known as:
o: Incremental budgeting.
Zero-base budgeting.
Performance budgeting.
Program budgeting.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting method in which the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget and the likely operating results for the current period. In zero-based budgeting the manager must start from scratch and justify all incomes and expenses proposed.

wrongWhy.0: Incremental budgeting simply takes historical information and adjusts it for anticipated increases or decreases in the coming year. This type of budgeting does not involve justifying the entire budget for each budget period.
wrongWhy.2: Performance budgets use statements of missions, goals and objectives to explain why the money is being spent. It is a way to allocate resources to achieve specific objectives based on program goals and measured results. Performance budgeting starts with a goal, then determines strategies and activities to accomplish that goal and justify the expenses. However, that justification is limited to the activities that accomplish a particular goal and does not extend to the entire budget.
wrongWhy.3: Program budgeting is used mainly by non-profit organizations and governmental bodies. It involves planning and budgeting for a specific program which may have not only expenses but income as well. If the program has income, the goal is to have the program income not only cover the program expenses but to exceed them. Program budgeting is limited to the activities that relate to one specific program and does not extend to the entire budget.

3-2.q: There are many different budget techniques or processes that business organizations can employ. One of these techniques or processes is zero-base budgeting, which is:
o: Using the prior year's budget as a base year and adjusting it based on the experiences of the prior year and the expectations for the coming year.
Budgeting from the ground up as though the budget process were being initiated for the first time.
Developing budgeted costs from clear-cut measured relationships between inputs and outputs.
Budgeting for cash inflows and outflows to time investments and borrowings in a way to maintain a bank account with a minimum balance.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. Within zero-based budgeting all of the activities that a department undertakes are identified and then prioritized. This is the manner in which costs are justified and supported. Also, because the manager needs to examine every single expenditure and activity within the department, he is more likely to develop an alternative and, hopefully cheaper, method of accomplishing the same thing.

wrongWhy.0: This is not the definition of zero-based budgeting. This is a description of incremental budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is not the definition of zero-based budgeting.
wrongWhy.3: This is not the definition of zero-based budgeting. This describes cash management.

3-2.q: A municipal government requires each department supervisor to submit an annual budget request stating the specific goals of the department and listing a series of "decision packages" relating to each goal. Each decision package describes a set of desired activities, the benefits of these activities, and the potential consequences of not performing the activities.
