Funds are allocated based on the estimated costs and benefits of each package. This is an example of
o: a static budget.
zero-base budgeting.
an imposed budget.
incremental budgeting.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. Within zero-based budgeting all of the activities that a department undertakes are identified and then prioritized. This is the manner in which costs are justified and supported. Also, because the manager needs to examine every single expenditure and activity within the department, he is more likely to develop an alternative and, hopefully cheaper, method of accomplishing the same thing.

wrongWhy.0: A static budget is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity.
wrongWhy.2: An imposed budget is budget that is prepared by top management without the participation of lower level management.
wrongWhy.3: Incremental budgeting is based on the prior period's budget.

3-2.q: A company’s board of directors has requested a full in-depth review of all budgeted items for next fiscal year’s operating budget. The controller of the company subsequently advised all business unit heads that the company will not automatically approve operating budget items for next fiscal year simply because they were approved in the past, and that all operating budget items for next fiscal year will need to be justified. Based on the above information, which one of the following budgeting systems is the company most likely using?
o: Flexible budgeting.
Activity-based budgeting.
Project budgeting.
Zero-based budgeting.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting system in which every year the company starts with zero for their budget. This means that all costs need to be justified every year and all revenues need to be specifically planned for. This is the method that is being used in this question.

wrongWhy.0: Flexible budgeting is not the budget method that is being described in this question.
wrongWhy.1: Activity-based budgeting is not what is being described in this question.
wrongWhy.2: This question does not describe project budgeting.

3-2.q: The use of standard costs in the budgeting process signifies that an organization has most likely implemented a
o: Static budget.
Flexible budget.
Zero-base budget.
Capital budget.
3-2.e: A flexible budget is a budget that is prepared for the actual activity level achieved during the period. This is done using the standard cost per unit. Therefore, if a company is using standard costs it may indicate that they are also using a flexible budget.

wrongWhy.0: A fixed budget, or static budget, is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity within the company. A static budget does not require the use of standard costs.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. The use of this form of budgeting does not require the use of standard costs.
wrongWhy.3: The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant and equipment) expenditures are planned. This budget is often prepared years in advance so that the company is able to obtain the necessary financing or accumulate the necessary cash to carry out its capital expansion plans. Although capital expansion plans that affect the budget being developed must be incorporated into that budget, there is no reason to say that use of standard costs in the budgeting process signifies that an organization has implemented a capital budget. To the extent that the capital budget is incorporated into the budget being developed, a capital budget is a part of any budget, whether it is a static budget, a flexible budget, or a zero-base budget.

3-2.q:
Based on past experience, a company has developed the following budget formula for estimating its shipping expenses. The company's shipments average 12 lbs. per shipment:
Shipping costs = $16,000 + ($0.50 × lbs. shipped)
The planned activity and actual activity regarding orders and shipments for the current month are given in the following schedule:
— Columns: | Planned | Actual
Sales orders: | 800 | 780
Shipments: | 800 | 820
Units shipped: | 8,000 | 9,000
Sales: | $120,000 | $144,000
Total pounds shipped: | 9,600 | 12,300
The actual shipping costs for the month amounted to $21,000. The appropriate monthly flexible budget allowance for shipping costs for the purpose of performance evaluation would be
3-2.e: A lot of information is given in this question, but much of it is not needed. A flexible budget is prepared for the actual level of activity using all of the budgeted variable costs per unit along with the budgeted total fixed cost as determined at the beginning of the year. The question gives the formula and gives the actual shipping volume as 12,300 pounds. The formula is: Shipping costs = $16,000 + ($0.50 x lbs. shipped) Putting the information given in the question into the formula results in the flexible budget shipping costs as follows: Shipping costs = $16,000 + ($0.50 × 12,300) = $22,150.

wrongWhy.1: This answer uses the number of orders instead of the number of pounds shipped. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: This answer uses the average number of pounds budgeted per shipment multiplied by the actual number of shipments instead of the number of pounds actually shipped. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: This answer uses the planned number of pounds to be shipped. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: A manufacturing firm has certain peak seasons, the summer season and the last two weeks of February. During these periods of increased output, the firm leases additional production equipment and hires additional temporary employees. Which one of the following budget techniques would best fit this firm’s needs?
o: Project budgeting because discrete financial information is available.
Static budgeting because it shows the changes in sales or production levels.
Zero-based budgeting because it is based on manager input.
Flexible budgeting because it allows for adjustments to the budget based on actual activity levels.
3-2.e: Flexible budgeting is the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. A flexible budget is prepared for the actual level of activity using all of the budgeted variable costs per unit along with the budgeted total fixed cost as determined at the beginning of the year. The flexible budget is the budget that would have been prepared for the actual level of activity for the period if the actual level of activity had been known in advance. Thus, flexible budgeting can adapt to changes in activity.

wrongWhy.0: Project budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Static budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: Which one of the following statements regarding the difference between a flexible budget and a static budget is correct?
o: A flexible budget is established by operating management, while a static budget is determined by top management.
A flexible budget usually includes budgeted variable revenue and cost amounts for a different level of activity from the level of activity used in the static budget.
A flexible budget includes only variable costs, whereas a static budget includes only fixed costs.
A flexible budget primarily is prepared for planning purposes, while a static budget is prepared for performance evaluation.
3-2.e: The static budget is based on the level of activity planned at the start of the budget period. A flexible budget is developed using the budgeted revenues and costs per unit of activity as developed for the static budget, but based on the level of actual activity achieved in the budget period. The major difference between a flexible budget and a static budget is the use of the actual activity level in the flexible budget, whereas the static budget uses the activity level planned at the beginning of the budget period.

wrongWhy.0: There are a number of methods of developing budgets: participative budgeting, bottomup budgeting, top-down approach, etc. Any level of management can establish either a flexible budget or a static budget or both.
wrongWhy.2: Flexible budgets and static budgets both include both variable and fixed costs.
wrongWhy.3: A flexible budget and a static budget are usually both prepared for planning and performance evaluation purposes.

3-2.q: The controller of Allenwood Steel Company has tasked the newly-hired budget manager with restructuring the company’s budget system. The controller would like her to gather information from each unit on their activities, and, with top management, examine each unit’s contributions to the company as a whole. Then, with the objective of eliminating inefficiency and waste, each unit’s budget for the following year will be determined without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results. This method of budget development is called
o: activity-based budgeting.
pro forma budgeting.
zero-based budgeting.
flexible budgeting.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting involves preparing the budget without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period. Every planned activity must be justified with a costbenefit analysis. Zero-based budgeting is in contrast to incremental budgeting, where budgets are developed by beginning with the current period’s actual or the current period’s budgeted figures and adjusting them for any changes anticipated in the coming period.

wrongWhy.0: Determining each unit’s budget for the following year without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results is not called activity-based budgeting. Activity-based budgeting involves developing budgets based on cost drivers, which are activities performed that cause costs to be incurred. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Determining each unit’s budget for the following year without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results is not called pro forma budgeting. There is no such thing as "pro forma budgeting." Pro forma financial statements are prepared for internal use in the planning process and contain projected amounts that are expected if a particular course of action is followed. Pro forma financial statements are used in order to see what the financial statements of the firm will look like if something under consideration or forecasted actually happens. The term “pro forma” is sometimes used to refer to the master budget financial statements, but use in that context is an inappropriate use of the term. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Determining each unit’s budget for the following year without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results is not called flexible budgeting. A flexible budget is based on the master budget, but it adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: The use of the master budget throughout the year as a constant comparison with actual results signifies that the master budget is also a
o: Static budget.
Capital budget.
Flexible budget.
Zero-base budget.
3-2.e: This is a description of a static budget because the static budget is prepared for only one level of activity (a certain level of sales).

wrongWhy.1: The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant and equipment) expenditures are planned. The capital budget is often prepared for several years in advance. The amounts in it that affect the current year's budget will be included in the current year's master budget; but the master budget is not also the capital budget.
wrongWhy.2: A flexible budget is prepared for the actual level of activity, so it is not constant throughout the year.
wrongWhy.3: In a zero-base budget the current year's budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. Not all master budgets are zero-base budgets, and the fact that the master budget is used to make comparisons with actual results does not mean that it must be a zero-based budget.

3-2.q: What do flexible budgets accommodate?
o: Changes in activity levels.
Changes in prices, by revising the static budget.
Changes in the inflation rate.
Changes in capacity use, which they are designed to evaluate.
3-2.e: A flexible budget is based on the static budget, but variable revenues and variable expenses are adjusted upward or downward to reflect the actual activity level. A flexible budget is more useful than a static budget because it allows the company to compare the actual results with the budgeted results that have been adjusted for the actual level of activity. Thus, it accommodates changes in activity levels.

wrongWhy.1: A flexible budget is based on the static budget, but variable revenues and variable expenses are adjusted upward or downward to reflect the actual activity level. The actual activity level might well change when prices change, but the flexible budget does not incorporate changes in prices in its budgeted amounts, only changes in volume.
wrongWhy.2: The inflation rate should be considered when any budget such as a static budget, a project budget, or a flexible budget is developed. A flexible budget does not specifically accommodate changes in the inflation rate.
wrongWhy.3: Flexible budgets are not used to evaluate the usage of capacity.

3-2.q: The major feature of zero-based budgeting is that it
o: evaluates each activity and determines whether it should be maintained as it is, reduced, or eliminated.
focuses on planned capital outlays for property, plant, and equipment.
uses the previous year's budget and adjusts it for inflation.
assumes all activities are legitimate and worthy of receiving budget increases to cover any increased costs.
3-2.e: Under zero-based budgeting, the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period. Every planned activity must be justified with a cost-benefit analysis. Because the budget is built up from zero, each manager must justify all of the expenses in his or her department. Zero-based budgeting enables the company to identify expenses that are not value adding or that should be reduced due to some development in production methods or something similar.

wrongWhy.1: Zero-based budgeting does not focus on planned capital outlays for property, plant, and equipment. The capital budget focuses on planned capital outlays for property, plant, and equipment.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting does not use the previous year’s budget adjusted for inflation. Under zero-based budgeting, the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period.
wrongWhy.3: Zero-based budgeting does not assume that all activities are legitimate and worthy of receiving budget increases to cover any increased costs. Zero-based budgeting is just the opposite: every planned activity must be justified with a cost-benefit analysis.

3-2.q: Country Ovens is a family restaurant chain. Due to an unexpected road construction project, traffic passing by the Country Ovens restaurant in Newtown has significantly increased. As a result, restaurant volume has similarly increased well beyond the level expected. Which type of budget would be most appropriate in helping the restaurant manager plan for restaurant labor costs?
o: Zero-based budget.
Rolling budget.
Flexible budget.
Activity-based budget.
3-2.e: A flexible budget would be the most appropriate budget, as it would allow management to measure operating success at any activity level experienced. A flexible budget can be adjusted upward for increased traffic and, if necessary, adjusted back down when the construction is completed, providing management with accurate budget variance information under a variety of circumstances.

wrongWhy.0: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting method in which the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget and the likely operating results for the current period. Although this approach would be appropriate when the next year's volume is expected to far exceed this year’s sales volume, it is still a static budget. And a static budget does not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.
wrongWhy.1: A rolling budget is a continual budget that adds one month as another month is completed. This type of budget is a static budget, and a static budget does not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.
wrongWhy.3: Activity-based budgeting is similar in concept to activity-based costing. Activities that drive the costs are identified, a budgeted level of activity for each of these drivers is determined based on a budgeted level of production, and budgeted amounts are developed based on the budgeted level of activity. This is a good basis for developing a budget, but since it depends upon a budgeted level of activity, the budget developed will be a static budget that will not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.

3-2.q: Which one of the following statements is correct concerning a flexible budget cost formula? Variable costs are stated
o: in total and fixed costs are stated in total.
per unit and fixed costs are stated per unit.
per unit and fixed costs are stated in total.
in total and fixed costs are stated per unit.
3-2.e: In a flexible budget, the variable revenues and costs are adjusted, using the planned per unit amounts multiplied by the actual activity, i.e., the number of units actually produced or actually sold. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula variable costs are stated on a per unit basis. Fixed costs, however, do not change with changes in level of activity, as long as the activity remains within the relevant range. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula fixed costs are stated as a total amount, not as a per unit amount.

wrongWhy.0: In a flexible budget, the variable revenues and costs are adjusted, using the planned per unit amounts multiplied by the actual activity, i.e., the number of units actually produced or actually sold. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula variable costs are stated on a per unit basis. Fixed costs, however, do not change with changes in level of activity, as long as the activity remains within the relevant range. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula fixed costs are stated as a total amount, not as a per unit amount.
wrongWhy.1: In a flexible budget, the variable revenues and costs are adjusted, using the planned per unit amounts multiplied by the actual activity, i.e., the number of units actually produced or actually sold. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula variable costs are stated on a per unit basis. Fixed costs, however, do not change with changes in level of activity, as long as the activity remains within the relevant range. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula fixed costs are stated as a total amount, not as a per unit amount.
wrongWhy.3: In a flexible budget, the variable revenues and costs are adjusted, using the planned per unit amounts multiplied by the actual activity, i.e., the number of units actually produced or actually sold. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula variable costs are stated on a per unit basis. Fixed costs, however, do not change with changes in level of activity, as long as the activity remains within the relevant range. Therefore, in a flexible budget cost formula fixed costs are stated as a total amount, not as a per unit amount.

3-2.q: A flexible budget is a quantitative expression of a plan that
o: is developed for the actual level of output achieved for the budget period.
focuses on the costs of activities necessary to produce and sell products and services for a budget period.
is comprised of the budgeted income statement and its supporting schedules for a budget period.
projects costs on the basis of future improvements in existing practices and procedures during a budget period.
3-2.e: This is the definition of a flexible budget.

wrongWhy.1: This is the definition of activity-based budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is the definition of an operating budget.
wrongWhy.3: This is the definition of Kaizen budgeting.

3-2.q: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is called a
o: rolling budget.
flexible budget.
strategic budget.
master budget.
3-2.e: The master budget expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year. The master budget is a full set of budgeted financial statements for the budget year, including monthly or at least quarterly interim budgeted financial statements. The budgeted financial statements include the budgeted balance sheet, budgeted income statement, and budgeted statement of cash flows. The master budget is created using both non-financial and financial assumptions, which come about as a result of the planning process. The master budget is a result of both operating decisions and financing decisions. Operating decisions are concerned with the best use of the company’s limited resources. Financing decisions are concerned with obtaining the funds to acquire the resources the company needs. The master budget is a static budget because each item in it is developed using an assumption that includes just one projected activity level.

wrongWhy.0: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a rolling budget. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a flexible budget. A flexible budget is based on the master budget, but it adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a strategic budget. A strategic budget is a budget that is aligned with and takes into consideration the entity's long term objectives that will take more than one year to achieve. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: In an organization that plans by using comprehensive budgeting, the master budget refers to:
o: the booklet containing budget guidelines, policies, and forms to use in the budgeting process.
a budget of a not-for-profit organization after it is approved by the appropriate authoritative body.
a compilation of all the separate operational and financial budget schedules of the organization.
the current budget updated for operations for part of the current year.
3-2.e: The individual budgets that make up the operating and financial budgets are compiled into a budgeted income statement, balance sheet, and statement of cash flows, which is the master budget.

wrongWhy.0: This does not describe the master budget.
wrongWhy.1: This does not describe the master budget.
wrongWhy.3: This does not describe the master budget.

3-2.q: A systemized approach known as zero-base budgeting (ZBB)
o: Divides the activities of individual responsibility centers into a series of packages that are prioritized.
Presents the plan for only one level of activity and does not adjust to changes in the level of activity.
Presents a statement of expectations for a period of time but does not present a firm commitment.
Focuses on the budgeted cost of activities necessary to produce products.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. This is quite different from a budget that is prepared by looking at the current year's actual or budgeted amounts and simply adjusting them (usually increasing them) for expected changes in the coming year. Though this method is more time consuming and difficult for all of the people involved, there are a number of advantages to the company as a result of using this method. Because the budget is built up from zero, each manager must justify all of the expenses in his or her department. Each component is evaluated from a cost-benefit perspective and priorities are determined. Zero-based budgeting enables the company to identify expenses that are not value adding or that should be reduced due to some development in production methods or something similar.

wrongWhy.1: This is a description of a static budget, not a zero-base budget. A static budget is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity within the company. A zero-base budget may be a static budget, but it may also be a flexible budget.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting represents a firm commitment to the company just like any other budget.
wrongWhy.3: Zero-based budgeting is applicable to all areas of the organization and includes all selling and administrative costs, not only costs for activities to produce products.

3-2.q: A flexible budget is appropriate for
o: Control of direct materials and direct labor but not selling and administrative expenses.
Any level of activity.
Control of fixed factory overhead but not direct materials and direct labor.
Control of direct labor and direct materials but not fixed factory overhead.
3-2.e: Fixed costs are the same for any level of activity within the relevant range. Thus, a flexible budget is not necessary to control fixed factory overhead. In fact, total fixed costs are the same in a flexible budget as they are in the static budget. However, a flexible budget is necessary to control direct materials and direct labor if the actual activity level differs from the static budget activity level.

wrongWhy.0: A flexible budget is necessary to control direct materials and direct labor as well as variable selling and administrative expenses if the actual activity level differs from the static budget activity level. To control fixed costs, the use of static budget is appropriate, not necessarily the use of flexible budget.
wrongWhy.1: While a flexible budget can be prepared for any level of activity, this is not the best answer to the question among the answer choices given.
wrongWhy.2: Fixed costs are the same for any level of activity within the relevant range. Thus, a flexible budget is not necessary to control fixed factory overhead. A flexible budget is necessary to control direct materials and direct labor if the actual activity level differs from static budget activity level.

3-2.q: Which of the following best describes a continuous profit plan?
o: An annual plan that is part of a 5-year plan.
A plan that is revised monthly or quarterly, always covering a constant period ahead.
A plan that works best for a company that can reliably forecast events a year or more into the future.
A plan devised by a full-time planning staff.
3-2.e: A continuous plan is one that is automatically prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous plan will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months.

wrongWhy.0: A continuous plan does not need to be part of a larger 5-year plan.
wrongWhy.2: A continuous plan works for any company, whether or not they can accurately forecast more than a year into the future.
wrongWhy.3: A continuous plan may or may not be produced by a full-time planning staff.

3-2.q: Which of the following describes a continuous (rolling) budget?
o: It presents the plan for only one level of activity and does not adjust to changes in the level of activity.
It drops the current month or quarter and adds a future month or quarter as the current month or quarter is completed.
It presents planned activities for a period but does not present a firm commitment.
It presents the plan for a range of activity so that the plan can be adjusted for changes in activity.
3-2.e: A continuous budget, also called a rolling budget, is one that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months.

wrongWhy.0: This is not the description of a rolling budget. "Continuous" or "rolling" merely identifies a budget that has been prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months. This is a description of a static budget. A static budget is developed for one specific activity level. A static budget may be in the form of a rolling budget, but it may also be an annual budget, that is, a budget prepared for a full fiscal year prior to the beginning of that fiscal year.
wrongWhy.2: This is not the description of a rolling budget. "Continuous" or "rolling" merely identifies a budget that has been prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months. No type of budget should present a firm commitment. A budget should be used as a guide, a motivating device, and a control tool; but it should be able to be revised if necessary. If conditions change during the budget period, the budget should not be used as an excuse for not doing something that is strategically important to the company, such as acting on an unforeseen business opportunity that arises. The statement “it’s not in the budget” should not be used.
wrongWhy.3: This is not the description of a rolling budget. "Continuous" or "rolling" merely identifies a budget that has been prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months. No type of budgeting presents the plan for a range of activity so the plan can be adjusted for changes in activity, but this is close to the description of a flexible budget. A flexible budget takes the variable revenues and costs as they are planned in the master budget (the static budget) and adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity levels had been used in preparing the budget. A flexible budget can be prepared only after the actual level of activity is known. A flexible budget may be in the form of a rolling budget, but it may also be an annual budget, that is, a budget prepared for a full fiscal year prior to the beginning of that fiscal year.

3-2.q: A continuous budget is one that
o: is available for a specified future period by adding a period to the period that just ended.
is created after the organization has been operating for at least one period.
is adjusted throughout the period for changing environmental factors.
uses the prior period's actual results as the current period's budget.
3-2.e: At all times, a continuous budget covers a set number of months, quarters, or years into the future. Each month or quarter, the month or quarter just completed is dropped and a new monthly or quarterly budget is added to the end of the budget.

wrongWhy.1: A continuous budget can be created at any time, not just after the organization has been operating for at least one period.
wrongWhy.2: A continuous budget is not adjusted throughout the period for changing environmental factors.
wrongWhy.3: A continuous budget does not use the prior period’s actual results as the current period’s budget.

3-2.q: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is a
o: sales budget.
flexible budget.
rolling budget.
master budget.
3-2.e: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is a rolling budget. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months.

wrongWhy.0: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a sales budget. The sales budget is the first operating budget to be prepared in the process of developing the master budget for the year. It incorporates the expected sales in units of each product and each product’s expected selling price. The sales budget is based on the firm’s forecasted sales level, its short- and long-term objectives, and its production capacity. Since it is part of the development of the master budget, the sales budget is developed using just one projected sales level. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a flexible budget. A flexible budget is a budget in which the master budget amounts have been adjusted to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a master budget. A master budget is a budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year, and each item in it is developed using an assumption that includes just one projected activity level. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: A company prepares a budget covering the next twelve months. At the end of each month it drops the month just completed and adds the same month of the following year, so that twelve months are always budgeted. This budget is best described as
o: A static budget.
A continuous (rolling) budget.
A zero-based budget.
A project budget.
3-2.e: A continuous, or rolling, budget always keeps a constant number of future periods in view. As each period closes it is dropped and a new period is added at the far end, so management is never looking at a budget horizon that shrinks as the year progresses.

wrongWhy.0: A static budget is prepared for one planned level of activity and is not changed during the period. The defining feature here is the rolling horizon, not the level of activity.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting describes how each amount is justified — every item is built up from zero each period. It says nothing about how far ahead the budget extends.
wrongWhy.3: A project budget follows a single project from beginning to end. It is not a recurring twelve-month horizon that rolls forward each month.

3-2.q: In deciding whether to investigate a variance, management should primarily consider
o: Whether the variance is favorable or unfavorable.
Whether the expected benefit of the investigation exceeds its cost.
The size of the department that reported the variance.
Whether the variance arose in a fixed cost or a variable cost.
3-2.e: Investigating a variance consumes management and accounting time, so it is itself a cost. A variance is worth investigating only when the expected benefit — correcting a problem, updating an out-of-date standard, preventing a recurrence — is greater than the cost of finding out what happened.

wrongWhy.0: Direction alone is not the test. A favorable variance can signal an obsolete standard or a shortcut that will cause problems later, and a small unfavorable variance may not be worth pursuing.
wrongWhy.2: Department size does not determine whether an investigation pays for itself. A small department can produce a variance that matters, and a large one can produce a trivial variance.
wrongWhy.3: Both fixed and variable costs produce variances worth investigating. The cost behavior pattern helps explain the variance but does not decide whether to look into it.

3-2.q: Which one of the following is the best reason for investigating a favorable variance?
o: Favorable variances never need to be investigated.
It may signal an out-of-date standard, or a quality shortcut that will create problems in a later period.
It automatically indicates good performance that should be rewarded.
It removes the need to prepare a flexible budget.
3-2.e: A favorable variance is not automatically good news. It may mean the standard is too loose and no longer reflects current conditions, or that a cost was avoided in a way that stores up trouble — cheaper materials that raise the defect rate, or deferred maintenance that causes downtime later.

wrongWhy.0: Treating favorable variances as automatically acceptable is exactly the mistake this reasoning warns against. Investigation criteria should be applied to variances in both directions.
wrongWhy.2: Sometimes a favorable variance does reflect good performance, but that is a conclusion an investigation reaches, not an assumption made before one.
wrongWhy.3: The flexible budget is what makes a meaningful variance possible in the first place, by restating the budget at the actual level of activity. Nothing about a favorable variance removes that need.

3-2.q:
A company investigates any cost variance greater than $10,000 or 5% of the budgeted amount, whichever is lower. Based on the data below, which item must be investigated?
Item — Columns: | Budgeted cost | Variance
Item A: | $150,000 | $8,000
Item B: | $400,000 | $9,000
Item C: | $60,000 | $2,500
Item D: | $900,000 | $9,500
o: Item A.
Item B.
Item C.
Item D.
3-2.e: For each item the threshold is the lower of $10,000 and 5% of the budgeted amount. Item A: 5% of $150,000 is $7,500, which is lower than $10,000, so the threshold is $7,500 and the $8,000 variance exceeds it. Item A must be investigated.

wrongWhy.1: For Item B, 5% of $400,000 is $20,000, so the lower threshold is $10,000. The $9,000 variance falls below it.
wrongWhy.2: For Item C, 5% of $60,000 is $3,000, which is the lower threshold. The $2,500 variance falls below it.
wrongWhy.3: For Item D, 5% of $900,000 is $45,000, so the lower threshold is $10,000. The $9,500 variance falls just below it.

3-2.q: A small unfavorable direct materials usage variance has appeared in each of the last six months. Management should
o: Ignore it, because each individual month's amount is immaterial.
Investigate it, because a variance that persists in the same direction suggests the standard or the process is wrong.
Write the accumulated amount off to cost of goods sold.
Revise the flexible budget so that it matches actual results.
3-2.e: Materiality is not judged one month at a time. A variance that recurs in the same direction period after period is evidence of something systematic — an outdated standard, a change in material quality, or a process that no longer performs as assumed — and the accumulated effect over a year can be large.

wrongWhy.0: Judging each month in isolation is what allows a persistent problem to escape attention. The pattern itself is the signal, regardless of the size of any single month.
wrongWhy.2: Writing the amount off records the effect in the accounts but does nothing about the cause, which will keep producing the same variance next month.
wrongWhy.3: Restating the budget to agree with actual results eliminates the variance on paper and destroys the budget's value as a control tool. The point of the comparison is to expose differences, not hide them.

3-2.q: Which one of the following is least likely to justify investigating a variance?
o: The variance is large in both dollar terms and percentage terms.
The variance has recurred for several consecutive periods.
The variance arose in a cost that no one in the organization is able to control.
The variance arose in a cost that is critical to product quality.
3-2.e: An investigation is worthwhile only if it can lead to action. When a cost is genuinely outside anyone's control — a tax rate change or a commodity price set in a world market — investigating explains the number but cannot change the outcome, so the expected benefit is small.

wrongWhy.0: Size in both absolute and relative terms is the most common trigger for investigation, because a variance that is large on both measures is unlikely to be random.
wrongWhy.1: A recurring variance points to a systematic cause such as an out-of-date standard, and investigating it can prevent the same difference arising again.
wrongWhy.3: A variance in a cost critical to quality deserves attention even when small, because the underlying cause may affect the product itself and reach the customer.

3-2.q: A project budget differs from the annual operating budget principally because a project budget
o: Covers exactly one accounting period.
Follows a specific project from beginning to end, often crossing departments and more than one accounting period.
Is prepared only for capital expenditures.
Must always be developed using zero-based budgeting.
3-2.e: A project budget is organized around a project rather than around a period or a department. It covers the whole life of the project, from initiation to completion, and gathers the costs from every department involved, so it commonly spans several accounting periods.

wrongWhy.0: That describes the annual operating budget. The project budget is defined by the project's own timeline, which rarely coincides with a single fiscal year.
wrongWhy.2: Many project budgets do involve capital spending, but a project budget can equally cover a marketing campaign, a system implementation or a research programme with no capital asset at all.
wrongWhy.3: Zero-based budgeting is a technique for justifying amounts. A project budget may or may not use it; the two ideas are independent.

3-2.q: Which situation is best suited to project budgeting?
o: Budgeting the monthly payroll of the accounting department.
Budgeting the construction of a new distribution centre expected to take eighteen months.
Budgeting routine annual maintenance costs.
Budgeting monthly sales by geographic region.
3-2.e: The construction has a defined beginning and end, spans more than one fiscal year, and draws on several departments. Those are exactly the conditions a project budget is designed for: costs are accumulated and controlled by project rather than by period.

wrongWhy.0: Departmental payroll is a recurring periodic cost with no defined end point. It belongs in the department's annual operating budget.
wrongWhy.2: Routine annual maintenance recurs every year and is planned by period, which is what the operating budget does.
wrongWhy.3: Monthly sales by region is periodic, recurring reporting. It fits the sales budget, not a project budget.

3-2.q: Life-cycle budgeting estimates
o: Only the manufacturing costs of a product.
The revenues and costs of a product over its entire life, from research and development through to withdrawal from the market.
Costs for the current fiscal year only.
The remaining useful lives of the company's fixed assets.
3-2.e: Life-cycle budgeting looks at a product across its whole life rather than one year at a time. It brings research and development, design, production, marketing, distribution and customer service costs into a single picture so management can judge whether the product will be profitable overall.

wrongWhy.0: Manufacturing costs are only one stage. Confining the analysis to production hides the upstream design spending and the downstream service costs that often decide whether a product pays.
wrongWhy.2: A single fiscal year is what the annual operating budget covers. Life-cycle budgeting exists precisely because one year is too short a window for products with long development or service tails.
wrongWhy.3: That describes fixed asset records and depreciation schedules, which concern assets rather than products.

3-2.q: Life-cycle budgeting is most useful for a product that
o: Has been sold unchanged for many years.
Requires heavy research, development and design spending before the first unit is sold.
Has no fixed costs.
Is purchased finished and resold without modification.
3-2.e: When a large share of a product's total cost is committed before any revenue arrives, judging the product on one year's manufacturing costs is misleading. Life-cycle budgeting spreads the development and design spending across the whole life so the product's true profitability becomes visible.

wrongWhy.0: A mature product with stable costs and a long history is adequately handled by the ordinary annual budget, because each year looks much like the last.
wrongWhy.2: Every product carries some fixed costs, and the absence of fixed costs would not be the reason for choosing a life-cycle view.
wrongWhy.3: A reseller incurs no development or design costs, so the upstream stages that make life-cycle budgeting valuable are absent.

3-2.q: Which cost would be included in a product's life-cycle budget but excluded from the conventional product cost reported for a single year?
o: Direct materials used during the current year.
Product design costs incurred two years before launch and customer-support costs incurred after the final sale.
Factory rent for the current year.
Direct labor incurred during the current year.
3-2.e: The life-cycle budget captures every cost caused by the product, including the upstream costs incurred before the product reaches the market and the downstream costs incurred after the last unit is sold. Conventional annual product costing recognizes neither.

wrongWhy.0: Direct materials used in the current year are part of the conventional product cost for that year, so they appear in both views.
wrongWhy.2: Current-year factory rent is part of manufacturing overhead in the conventional annual cost, so it is not unique to the life-cycle view.
wrongWhy.3: Current-year direct labor is a conventional product cost and appears in both the annual figures and the life-cycle budget.

3-2.q: A company is preparing a life-cycle budget for a new product. Research and development is budgeted at $600,000, design at $200,000, marketing and distribution at $400,000, and customer service at $150,000. Manufacturing cost is budgeted at $18 per unit, and 100,000 units are expected to be sold over the product's life. The budgeted life-cycle cost per unit is
3-2.e: The costs that do not vary with units are added together and spread over the expected life volume: $600,000 + $200,000 + $400,000 + $150,000 = $1,350,000, divided by 100,000 units, is $13.50 per unit. Adding the $18.00 manufacturing cost per unit gives a life-cycle cost of $31.50 per unit.

wrongWhy.0: $18.00 is the manufacturing cost per unit alone. Using it as the life-cycle cost ignores the $1,350,000 of development, design, marketing and service costs the product also causes.
wrongWhy.2: $13.50 is only the non-manufacturing portion spread over the life volume. The manufacturing cost per unit must be added to it.
wrongWhy.3: $30.00 results from omitting one of the four non-manufacturing cost pools. All four belong in the life-cycle budget.

3-2.q: A company budgets variable costs of $6 per unit and fixed costs of $120,000 for the year, based on planned production of 40,000 units. Actual production was 45,000 units. The flexible budget total cost for the actual level of production is
3-2.e: The flexible budget applies the budgeted rates to the actual level of activity. Variable costs become 45,000 units × $6 = $270,000. Fixed costs stay at the static budget amount of $120,000, because fixed costs do not flex within the relevant range. The flexible budget total is $390,000.

wrongWhy.0: $360,000 is the static budget: 40,000 units × $6 plus $120,000 of fixed cost. That is the original plan, not the budget restated at actual volume.
wrongWhy.2: $405,000 results from flexing the fixed costs as well, by applying a combined $9 per unit rate to 45,000 units. Fixed costs must remain at $120,000.
wrongWhy.3: $270,000 is the variable cost component only. The fixed costs still have to be added.

3-2.q: A company's static budget was based on sales of 10,000 units with a budgeted contribution margin of $12 per unit. Actual sales were 11,000 units and the actual contribution margin earned was $126,500. The flexible budget variance for contribution margin is
o: $6,500 favorable.
$5,500 unfavorable.
$12,000 favorable.
$5,500 favorable.
3-2.e: The flexible budget contribution margin is 11,000 units × $12 = $132,000. The flexible budget variance compares actual results with the flexible budget: $126,500 − $132,000 = $5,500 unfavorable. The company sold more units than planned but earned less contribution per unit than budgeted.

wrongWhy.0: $6,500 is the difference between actual contribution margin of $126,500 and the static budget of $120,000. That combines the volume effect with the rate effect instead of separating them.
wrongWhy.2: $12,000 favorable is the sales volume variance: the flexible budget of $132,000 less the static budget of $120,000. It measures the effect of selling 1,000 extra units, not the flexible budget variance.
wrongWhy.3: The direction is wrong. Actual contribution margin of $126,500 is below the flexible budget of $132,000, so the variance is unfavorable.

3-2.q: A company budgeted a selling price of $25 per unit and planned to sell 8,000 units. It actually sold 8,600 units at an average price of $24. Flexible budget revenue is
3-2.e: The flexible budget uses the budgeted selling price at the actual sales volume: 8,600 units × $25 = $215,000. Comparing that figure with actual revenue of $206,400 isolates the effect of the price shortfall from the effect of selling more units than planned.

wrongWhy.0: $200,000 is the static budget revenue, 8,000 units × $25. It has not been restated for the actual volume of 8,600 units.
wrongWhy.1: $206,400 is actual revenue, 8,600 units × the actual $24 price. The flexible budget must use the budgeted price, not the actual one.
wrongWhy.3: $204,000 does not correspond to any meaningful combination of the budgeted and actual prices and volumes.

3-2.q: Which one of the following is part of the financial budget rather than the operating budget?
o: The direct labor usage budget.
The budgeted balance sheet.
The nonmanufacturing budgets.
The budgeted income statement.
3-2.e: The master budget has two halves. The operating budget is the budgeted income statement together with every schedule that supports it. The financial budget is the capital expenditures budget, the cash budget, the budgeted balance sheet and the budgeted statement of cash flows.

wrongWhy.0: The direct labor usage budget supports the cost of goods manufactured and therefore the budgeted income statement, placing it in the operating budget.
wrongWhy.2: The nonmanufacturing budgets — research and development, selling and distribution, administrative — feed the budgeted income statement and belong to the operating budget.
wrongWhy.3: The budgeted income statement is the end point of the operating budget, not part of the financial budget.

3-2.q: The operating budget culminates in the
o: Cash budget.
Budgeted income statement.
Budgeted balance sheet.
Capital expenditures budget.
3-2.e: Every schedule in the operating budget — sales, production, materials, labor, overhead, inventories, cost of goods manufactured, cost of goods sold and the nonmanufacturing budgets — feeds into the budgeted income statement, which is where the operating budget ends.

wrongWhy.0: The cash budget belongs to the financial budget. It is the last budget prepared before the budgeted financial statements, but it is not the end point of the operating budget.
wrongWhy.2: The budgeted balance sheet is part of the financial budget and is prepared after the cash budget.
wrongWhy.3: The capital expenditures budget is part of the financial budget and is prepared years in advance, before the annual budget cycle begins.

3-2.q: A master budget is best described as
o: A budget covering the manufacturing function only.
A comprehensive set of budgets covering all phases of operations, made up of the operating budget and the financial budget.
A budget that is revised whenever actual volume differs from the planned volume.
The company's long-range strategic plan.
3-2.e: The master budget, also called the comprehensive or annual budget, is the company's complete financial plan for the period. It consists of the operating budget, ending in the budgeted income statement, and the financial budget, ending in the budgeted balance sheet and budgeted statement of cash flows.

wrongWhy.0: The master budget covers selling, administrative, research, financing and investing activities as well as manufacturing.
wrongWhy.2: A budget restated at the actual level of activity is a flexible budget. The master budget is prepared at one planned level and is therefore a static budget.
wrongWhy.3: The strategic plan sets goals and direction over the long term. The master budget translates the near-term portion of that plan into quantified financial statements.

3-3.q: When sales volume is seasonal in nature, certain items in the budget must be coordinated. The three most significant items to coordinate in budgeting seasonal sales volume are
o: Raw material inventory, direct labor hours, and manufacturing overhead costs.
Raw material inventory, work-in-process inventory, and production volume.
Production volume, finished goods inventory, and sales volume.
Direct labor hours, work-in-process inventory, and sales volume.
3-3.e: Budgets usually start with the development of the sales budget, and it is the most difficult budget to prepare. Production volume and finished goods inventory budgets are the next budgets to be prepared, and their development is based on the assumptions made in preparing the sales budget. When the business is seasonal in nature it is even more difficult to predict sales volume and subsequent budgets. Thus, for any type of business including seasonal it is critical to coordinate production volume, finished goods inventory, and sales volume first of all.

wrongWhy.0: The sales budget is usually the first thing to determine in the budgeting process of any type of business including a seasonal business. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.1: The sales budget is usually the first thing to determine in the budgeting process of any type of business including a seasonal business. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.3: Direct labor hours, work-in-process inventory are elements within the production budget. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

3-3.q: Myers Company uses a calendar-year and prepares a cash budget for each month of the year. Which one of the following items should be considered when developing July's cash budget?
o: Property taxes levied in the last calendar year scheduled to be paid quarterly in the coming year during the last month of each calendar quarter.
Quarterly cash dividends scheduled to be declared on July 15 and paid on August 6 to shareholders of record as of July 25.
Federal income tax and social security tax withheld from employees' June paychecks to be remitted to the Internal Revenue Service in July.
Recognition that 0.5% of the July sales on account will be credit losses.
3-3.e: The payment of federal income tax and social security tax withheld from employees' June paychecks to be remitted to the Internal Revenue Service in July will affect July cash because it is a July disbursement to be made. Therefore, it should be considered when developing July’s cash budget.

wrongWhy.0: The payments of property taxes will be made during the last month of each calendar quarter. The last months of each calendar quarter are March, June, September and December. Since a quarterly property tax payment will not be made during July, it should not be considered when developing July's cash budget.
wrongWhy.1: The quarterly cash dividend will be accrued in July. However, it will not be paid until August, so it will not affect cash during July. Therefore, it should not be considered when developing July's cash budget.
wrongWhy.3: The allowance for credit losses is recorded by debiting credit loss expense and crediting the allowance for credit losses account. Since it is not a cash transaction, it should not be considered when developing July's cash budget.

3-3.q: When budgeting, the items to be considered by a manufacturing firm in going from a sales quantity budget to a production budget would be the
o: Expected change in the quantity of work-in-process inventories only.
Expected change in the quantity of finished goods and work-in-process inventories.
Expected change in the availability of raw material without regard to inventory levels.
Expected change in the quantity of finished goods and raw material inventories.
3-3.e: Developing the production budget involves deciding what quantities should be manufactured during a period. The expected amount of sales from the sales budget for the period is used; and the expected amount of change in the level of finished goods inventories must also be considered. If a significant change in the level of work-in-process inventories is expected during the period, that amount of change should also be considered.

wrongWhy.0: The expected change in the level of work-in-process inventories should be considered in the development of the production budget if a significant change is expected during the period. However, the expected change in finished goods inventories must be considered as well in the development of the production budget.
wrongWhy.2: Expected changes in the availability of raw materials is a consideration in developing the direct materials purchases budget, which is developed from the production budget, but it is not used in developing the production budget. Furthermore, inventory levels must be a consideration in developing the production budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: The expected change in raw materials inventories is used in developing the direct materials purchases budget, not the production budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-3.q: The most direct way to prepare a cash budget for a manufacturing firm is to include
o: Projected net income, depreciation, and goodwill impairment.
Projected sales, credit terms, and net income.
Projected sales and purchases, percentages of collections, and terms of payments.
Projected purchases, percentages of purchases paid, and net income.
3-3.e: All of these items listed are related to cash and would therefore be included in the preparation of a cash budget.

wrongWhy.0: Projected net income is not included in the cash budget because it includes noncash items like depreciation and the impairment of goodwill. Depreciation and goodwill impairment are not included because they are noncash items.
wrongWhy.1: Net income is not included in the cash budget because it includes noncash items like depreciation and the amortization of bond premium.
wrongWhy.3: Net income is not included in the cash budget because it includes noncash items like depreciation and the amortization of bond premium.

3-3.q: In developing a comprehensive annual budget for a manufacturing company, which one of the following items should be done first?
o: Determination of manufacturing capacity.
Determination of the advertising budget.
Development of a sales plan.
Development of the capital budget.
3-3.e: The sales plan/forecast is usually prepared first in the budgeting process. The production budget and all the other budgets for the company are derived from the sales budget.

wrongWhy.0: Determination of manufacturing capacity is a part of the process of developing the sales budget as well as the production budget. However, determination of manufacturing capacity is not necessarily the first thing to be done in developing a comprehensive budget for a manufacturing company.
wrongWhy.1: The advertising budget is one of the components of the operating budget. The advertising budget is one of the nonmanufacturing budgets, but it is not the first budget to be developed. It is derived from other operating budgets that must be developed first.
wrongWhy.3: The capital budget is usually prepared for periods of several years. Any planned projects affecting the next year's budget need to be included in the budget. The capital budget is not developed along with the next year's budget, though, because it is outside of the current year's budget. So it would not be the first budget to be developed.

3-3.q: In the budgeting and planning process for a firm, which one of the following should be completed first?
o: Financial budget
Sales budget
Strategic plan
Cost management plan
3-3.e: The strategic plan must be in place before any budgeting activities can occur. Though the sales budget is the first budget that needs to be completed, in the budgeting and planning process the strategic plan comes before the sales budget.

wrongWhy.0: Preparing the financial budget is not the first step to be completed in the budgeting and planning process.
wrongWhy.1: Preparing the sales budget is not the first step to be completed in the budgeting and planning process.
wrongWhy.3: The cost management plan is not the first step to be completed in the budgeting and planning process.

3-3.q: A company operates on a calendar year but maintains four continuous quarterly budgets for up-to-date budget information. After completing its five-year long-term plan, the company’s management decided to make a capital investment in new equipment. The equipment is expected to have a useful life of nine years. Which one of the following is the most appropriate time period for the capital budget for the evaluation of the capital investment?
o: Five years.
Nine years.
Four continuous quarters.
One year.
3-3.e: The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.

wrongWhy.0: The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.
wrongWhy.2: The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.
wrongWhy.3: The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.

3-3.q: Which one of the following schedules would be the last item to be prepared in the normal budget preparation process?
o: Cost of goods sold budget.
Manufacturing overhead budget.
Cash budget.
Direct labor budget.
3-3.e: Of all the budgets given in the answer choices, the cash budget is the last budget to be prepared because all other budgets are inputs to it.

wrongWhy.0: The cost of goods sold budget is developed after the production budget. It is a part of the operating budget but it is not the last budget to be prepared.
wrongWhy.1: The manufacturing overhead budget is part of the operating budget. The manufacturing overhead budget is developed after the production budget, but it is not the last budget to be prepared.
wrongWhy.3: The direct labor budget is part of the operating budget. The direct labor budget is developed after the production budget is developed, but it is not the last budget to be prepared.

3-3.q: Which one of the following items should be done first when developing a comprehensive budget for a manufacturing company?
o: Development of the cash budget.
Determination of the advertising budget.
Development of the sales budget.
Preparation of a budgeted income statement.
3-3.e: The sales budget is the first budget that needs to be set because the production budget and all the other budgets for the company are derived from the sales budget.

wrongWhy.0: The cash budget is not the first budget prepared.
wrongWhy.1: The advertising budget is not the first budget prepared.
wrongWhy.3: The budgeted income statement is not the first budget prepared.

3-3.q: Which one of the following may be considered an independent item in the preparation of the master budget?
o: Ending inventory budget.
Capital investment budget.
Budgeted income statement.
Budgeted statement of financial position.
3-3.e: The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant, and equipment) expenditures are planned. The capital expenditures budget is often prepared years in advance so that the company can plan to obtain the necessary financing or accumulate the necessary cash to carry out its capital expansion plans. The capital budget is an independent item in the development of the master budget. It is not a part of the annual budget development process. However, the capital budget is very important to the development of the annual master budget. The capital expenditures budget for the period covering the budget year must be in place before the master budget can be developed because the master budget needs to incorporate the capital expenditures planned for the budget year.

wrongWhy.0: The desired level of ending inventory not independent, as it is used in the development of the production budget.
wrongWhy.2: The budgeted income statement is a critical element of the master budget. It is one of the last budgets created from the operating budgets. The budgeted income statement is based on the sales budgets, expense budgets and other elements of the master budget, so it is not an independent item in the preparation of the master budget.
wrongWhy.3: The budgeted statement of financial position (balance sheet) is based on a number of elements of the master budget including the budgeted income statement, so it is not an independent item in the preparation of the master budget.

3-3.q: Which one of the following statements regarding selling and administrative budgets is most accurate?
o: Selling and administrative budgets are difficult to allocate by month and are best presented as one number for the entire year.
Selling and administrative budgets are usually optional.
Selling and administrative budgets are fixed in nature.
Selling and administrative budgets need to be detailed so that the key assumptions can be better understood.
3-3.e: Selling and administrative budgets should be detailed enough to be useful, including an explanation of the underlying assumptions. These assumptions should be documented so that if they are changed it will be easier to adjust the affected budget figures.

wrongWhy.0: Selling and administrative budgets are not difficult to allocate by month and are not necessarily best presented as one number for the entire year.
wrongWhy.1: Selling and administrative budgets are not usually optional.
wrongWhy.2: Selling and administrative budgets are not fixed in nature.

3-3.q: Which of the following statements about the master budget is correct?
o: It reflects controllable costs only.
It can be used to determine manufacturing cost variances.
It contains the operating budgets.
It shows forecasted and actual results.
3-3.e: The master budget includes the operating budget, which consists of the budgeted income statement and all the budgets that support the budgeted income statement. The master budget also includes the financial budget, which consists of the capital expenditures budget, the cash budget, the budgeted balance sheet, and the budgeted statement of cash flows.

wrongWhy.0: The master budget is prepared for one level of activity and is developed before the budget period. The master budget consists of the financial budget and the operating budget. The operating budget includes all budgeted expenses whether or not they are controllable by specific, individual managers.
wrongWhy.1: The master budget cannot be used to determine manufacturing cost variances. The master budget is prepared for one level of activity. Manufacturing variances are determined by comparing actual results with flexible budget amounts. A flexible budget is a budget that is prepared using the standard (budgeted) costs per unit produced and the actual level of production. The flexible budget is essentially what the budget would have been if the company had known what the actual level of production would be in advance and had used that information when it developed the budget.
wrongWhy.3: The master budget is prepared for one level of activity and is developed before the budget period. The master budget consists of the financial budget and the operating budget. The master budget does not include actual results.

3-3.q: Playtime Toys estimates that it will sell 200,000 dolls during the coming year. The beginning inventory is 12,000 dolls; the target ending inventory is 15,000 dolls. Each doll requires two shoes which are purchased from an outside supplier. The beginning inventory of shoes is 20,000; the target ending inventory is 18,000 shoes. The number of shoes that should be purchased during the year is
3-3.e: The question tells us that beginning finished goods inventory of dolls on hand is 12,000 dolls and the target ending units of finished dolls in inventory is 15,000 dolls. The company plans to sell 200,000 dolls. The inventory formula is: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced/Purchased − Units Sold/Used = Ending Inventory Letting P stand for Units Produced, we have the following formula: Beginning Inventory of 12,000 finished dolls + P − 200,000 dolls sold = Ending Inventory of 15,000 dolls. Solving for P, we get P = 203,000 dolls. Since each doll produced requires 2 shoes, the total shoes required for production of 203,000 dolls = 203,000 × 2 = 406,000 shoes. We now use the shoes needed for current production along with the beginning and ending inventories of shoes to calculate the number of shoes that will need to be purchased. We use the same inventory formula: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced/Purchased − Units Sold/Used = Ending Inventory. Letting P stand for Purchases of shoes, we have the following formula: Beginning Inventory of 20,000 shoes + P − 406,000 shoes Used in Production = Ending Inventory of 18,000 shoes.

wrongWhy.0: This answer results from using the number of dolls to be sold instead of the number to be produced in calculating the number of shoes needed.
wrongWhy.1: This answer results from reversing the beginning and ending inventories when calculating both the number of dolls to be produced and the number of shoes to be purchased.
wrongWhy.2: This answer results from calculating Purchases using the following formula for shoes materials inventory: 20,000 Beginning Inventory + Purchases - 203,000 Used in Production = 18,000 Ending Inventory . . . and solving for Purchases and then doubling the Purchases figure that results. However, the number of shoes used in production is incorrect, as 203,000 is the number of dolls to be produced and each doll requires two shoes. Doubling the Purchases figure that results from that formula does not result in the correct number of shoes to be purchased.

Beginning A/R + sales made on account − receivables collected = ending A/R. Whenever we know three of these four amounts, we can calculate the fourth one. We know beginning A/R ($180,000), budgeted sales made on account ($800,000), and budgeted ending A/R ($210,000). Therefore, we can calculate that budgeted receivables collected = $770,000. 3-3.q: A firm develops an annual cash budget in order to
o: Avoid the opportunity costs of non-invested excess cash and minimize the cost of interim financing.
Determine the opportunity costs of alternative sales and production strategies.
Ascertain which capital expenditure projects are feasible and which capital expenditure projects should be deferred.
Support the preparation of its cash flow statement for the annual report.
3-3.e: The cash budget (or cash management and working capital budget) tracks the inflows and outflows of cash on a month-by-month (possibly even week-by-week or day-by-day) basis. If this budget is accurate it will allow the company to plan for any cash shortfalls that may occur during the year and also enable it to plan for any excess cash accumulating during the year. This can enable the company to plan its short-term investments of excess cash so that they mature when the cash is needed. And prediction of cash shortfalls enables the company to obtain a loan more easily because it is aware of its need before the shortfall arrives, and it can present cash inflow and outflow projections to the bank to support its loan request.

wrongWhy.1: The cash budget does not determine the opportunity costs of alternative sales and production strategies.
wrongWhy.2: The cash budget does not ascertain which capital expenditure projects are feasible or should be deferred. It only shows the cash available for projects and other activities.
wrongWhy.3: The cash flow statement in the annual report is based on actual results, not on budgeted figures.

wrongWhy.0: The statement of cash flows is the last financial statement to be prepared in the budgeting process.
wrongWhy.1: The budget for cost of goods sold is prepared before the budgeted income statement and before any of financial budgets (capital budget, cash budget, budgeted balance sheet, budgeted statement of cash flows).
wrongWhy.2: The income statement is an input to the balance sheet, so the income statement is prepared before the balance sheet.

Using flexible budgeting, what are the total costs associated with producing and selling 110,000 units?
3-3.e: Assuming all variable costs per unit and all fixed costs in total will be the same for the budget period as they were for the last fiscal year, the flexible budget total costs will be: Direct labor ($200,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 $220,000 Direct materials ($100,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 110,000 Variable manufacturing overhead ($100,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 110,000 Variable selling and administrative ($50,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 55,000 Fixed manufacturing overhead 100,000 Fixed selling and administrative 100,000 Total costs $695,000

wrongWhy.2: $715,000 is the total costs reported for the last fiscal year when the company produced 100,000 units, divided by 100,000 and the result multiplied by 110,000. However, some of those costs were fixed costs. Fixed costs do not change with changes in the activity level, as long as the volume remains within the relevant range. Since the question does not state that a volume of 110,000 units is outside the relevant range (and does not give an increased total fixed cost figure for producing and selling 110,000 units), the only possible assumption is that fixed costs will not change in total. Therefore, it is not appropriate to increase the fixed costs for the increase in production and sales expected for the budget period.
wrongWhy.3: $650,000 is the total costs reported for the last fiscal year when the company produced 100,000 units. The company plans to produce and sell 110,000 units during the budget period, and variable expenses for 110,000 units will be greater in total than they were for 100,000 units.
wrongWhy.1: $450,000 is the variable cost to produce and sell 100,000 units. Total cost includes fixed costs as well as variable costs; and the flexible budget cost should be based on 110,000 units, not 100,000 units. Furthermore, the total cost to produce and sell 100,000 units during the last fiscal year was $650,000, and the cost to produce and sell 110,000 units during the budget period would not be $200,000 less than the cost to produce and sell 100,000 units.

3-3.q: The budget that is usually the most difficult to forecast is the
o: Production budget.
Sales budget.
Manufacturing overhead budget.
Expense budget.
3-3.e: The sales budget is usually the first budget to be prepared, and it influences all other budgets in the master budget. The volume of sales and level of sales revenue are difficult to forecast, as many external factors can influence this budget. The sales budget is based on a number of assumptions about the changing environment such as competitors' activities, consumer tastes and demand, prices, the general economy, government regulations, and many other factors that are outside the control of the company. Therefore, the sales budget is very difficult to develop.

wrongWhy.0: The production budget is based on the sales budget, and once the sales budget has been prepared and ending inventory objectives determined, the production budget is relatively easy to prepare.
wrongWhy.2: The preparation of the manufacturing overhead budget is fairly straight-forward after the production budget has been prepared, because it is based on the production budget.
wrongWhy.3: There is no such thing as an "expense budget." General and administrative expenses are budgeted as part of the operating budget, and they are based upon management's plans for the coming year. Selling expenses are also a part of the operating budget, and they are based on the sales budget.

wrongWhy.2: Knowing the behavior of business cycles, understanding seasonal variations in demand for the product, and using econometric models can be valuable when forecasting the required purchases of inventory.
wrongWhy.3: Knowing the behavior of business cycles, understanding seasonal variations in demand for the product, and using econometric models can be valuable when forecasting the required purchases of inventory.

Production plan.
Capital budget.

wrongWhy.0: Cost and expense budgets cannot be calculated until the sales volume is forecast.
wrongWhy.2: The production plan cannot be calculated until the sales volume is forecast.
wrongWhy.3: The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant and equipment) expenditures are planned. The capital expenditures budget is often prepared years in advance so that the company is able to obtain the necessary financing or accumulate the necessary cash to carry out its capital expansion plans. This budget is related to many of the current period operating budgets because they are affected by the capital projects planned for the budget year. However, it is not the foundation for the whole profit plan for the coming year.

Balance sheet.
